GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44123 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44123 - Title: fix(hooks): use session creation date for memory filename - State: OPEN - Author: litchi98 - Labels: size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - **Problem**: Memory files were using `/new` event timestamp for the date, causing incorrect dates when a session created on one day had `/new` called on another day. - **Why it matters**: Users lose chronological accuracy in their memory files, making it harder to track when events actually occurred. - **What changed**: Added `getSessionCreatedAt()` to read session file creation time with validation; `dateStr` and `timeStr` now derive from session creation time; HHMM slug still uses `event.timestamp` to prevent filename conflicts. - **What did NOT change**: Memory file format, LLM slug generation, batch mode behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related #44060 (previous attempt with issues) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Memory file date now correctly reflects session creation date instead of `/new` execution date - Header timestamp (`# Session: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC`) now uses consistent session creation time ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (arm64) - Runtime: Node.js v25.8.0 - Model: zai/glm-5 - Integration: webchat ### Steps 1. Create a session on Day 1 2. Call `/new` on Day 2 3. Check memory file date ### Expected Memory file date should be Day 1 (session creation date). ### Actual (before fix) Memory file date was Day 2 (`/new` execution date). ### Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after Test cases added: - `should use session creation date from session file header` - `should handle invalid date string in session header gracefully` - `should handle missing session file gracefully` - `should use event.timestamp for HHMM slug to avoid conflicts` - `should handle cross-minute boundary in tests correctly` ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: Local test suite (23 tests pass), manual inspection of generated memory files - Edge cases checked: Invalid date strings, missing session files, cross-minute boundaries - What you did **not** verify: Production environment with real sessions ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit or close PR - Files/config to restore: None - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Memory files not being created on `/new` ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: `getSessionCreatedAt()` reads session file which could fail on corrupted files - Mitigation: Try-catch with fallback to `event.timestamp` ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T14:34:40Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug where memory files used the `/new` execution date instead of the session creation date for their filename and header timestamp. A new `getSessionCreatedAt()` helper reads the `{type:"session", timestamp:...}` entry from the first line of the session JSONL file, with `dateStr` and `timeStr` now deriving from that value; the HHMM fallback slug continues to use `event.timestamp` to prevent filename collisions. **Key findings:** - **Logic gap — missing `.reset.*` fallback in `getSessionCreatedAt`**: `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback` falls back to `.reset.*` sibling files when the base session file is empty (which happens when a session has already been rotated at the time the hook fires). `getSessionCreatedAt` does not have this same fallback, so in the rotated-before-hook scenario the session creation date will silently fall back to `event.timestamp` even though the correct timestamp is present in the reset file's first line. This directly undermines the fix in a real production code path already covered by existing tests. - **Test flakiness in HHMM slug test**: `preRunTime` is captured before `runNewWithPreviousSessionEntry`, which internally calls `createHookEvent` → `new Date()`. At a minute boundary, the extracted HHMM will differ from `preRunTime`'s HHMM, causing a spurious failure. A post-run timestamp bracket would eliminate the race.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge for most cases, but the reset-file fallback gap means the fix doesn't work when sessions are rotated before the hook fires. - The core fix is correct and well-tested for the primary use case (session file intact at hook time), and fallback to `event.timestamp` is always graceful. However, the asymmetry between `getSessionCreatedAt` and `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback` — where message content is recovered from `.reset.*` files but the session creation date is not — means the fix silently fails for rotated sessions. Given that an existing test explicitly exercises the rotated-file scenario for message content, this gap is likely to surface in production. - Pay close attention to `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.ts` around the `getSessionCreatedAt` function and its call site — specifically whether it should mirror the `.reset.*` fallback logic of `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback`.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.ts Line: 303-305 Comment: **`getSessionCreatedAt` doesn't check `.reset.*` fallback files** There's an asymmetry between how the session creation timestamp and message content are retrieved. When `previousSessionEntry.sessionFile` points to a base file (`test-session.jsonl`) that has already been emptied by session rotation, `getSessionCreatedAt` returns `null` and falls back to `event.timestamp` — but `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback` correctly reads the messages from the corresponding `*.reset.*` file. This means for rotated sessions, the memory file will use the `/new` execution date instead of the session creation date, defeating the purpose of this fix. The existing test "falls back to latest .jsonl.reset.* transcript when active file is empty" already confirms this rotation scenario is expected in production. To fix the asymmetry, `getSessionCreatedAt` should similarly check `.reset.*` sibling files when the base file is empty or has no valid session header: ```ts if (sessionFile) { sessionCreatedAt = await getSessionCreatedAt(sessionFile); // If base file is empty (rotated), check the reset fallback files if (!sessionCreatedAt) { const dir = path.dirname(sessionFile); const base = path.basename(sessionFile); try { const siblingFiles = await fs.readdir(dir); const resetCandidates = siblingFiles .filter(name => name.startsWith(`${base}.reset.`)) .toSorted() .toReversed(); for (const candidate of resetCandidates) { sessionCreatedAt = await getSessionCreatedAt(path.join(dir, candidate)); if (sessionCreatedAt) break; } } catch { /* ignore */ } } } ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts Line: 662-676 Comment: **Minute-boundary race condition in HHMM slug test** `preRunTime` is captured before the hook event is created inside `runNewWithPreviousSessionEntry`. Because `createHookEvent` calls `new Date()` internally, if the handler starts just after `preRunTime` crosses a minute boundary (e.g., `preRunTime = 14:44:59`, event timestamp = `14:45:00`), `nowHHMM` will be `"1444"` while `hhmm` will be `"1445"`, causing a spurious failure. A more robust approach is to also compute `postRunTime` after the handler returns, and assert that the extracted HHMM falls within the two minute-values: ```ts const preRunTime = new Date(); const { files } = await runNewWithPreviousSessionEntry({ ... }); const postRunTime = new Date(); const hhmm = files[0].match(/2026-03-10-(\d{4})\.md$/)?.[1]; const preHHMM = preRunTime.toISOString().split("T")[1].split(":").slice(0, 2).join(""); const postHHMM = postRunTime.toISOString().split("T")[1].split(":").slice(0, 2).join(""); expect([preHHMM, postHHMM]).toContain(hhmm); ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44136 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44136 - Title: fix(copilot): respect user-configured baseUrl in token flow - State: OPEN - Author: AZDSoftware - Labels: agents, size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary **Real-world validation**: Setting `models.providers.github-copilot.baseUrl` to `https://api.business.githubcopilot.com` in openclaw.json eliminates 421 errors for Business accounts. This PR builds on PR #29614 by implementing the priority logic reviewer feedback identified: **user-configured baseUrl should take priority over token-derived baseUrl**. ## Changes - Add conditional baseUrl assignment in `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` (two locations: initial auth + token refresh) - Only apply token-derived baseUrl if: - Token provides one (which it should) - **AND** user hasn't configured a custom one (or it's still the default) This ensures: ✅ User-configured workaround (`baseUrl: "https://api.business.githubcopilot.com"`) continues to work ✅ Enterprise/Business accounts auto-correct when user hasn't configured anything ✅ Corporate proxy configs aren't overridden ✅ Backwards compatible (Individual accounts still work) ## Affected Accounts | Tier | Token `proxy-ep` | Current (broken) | After PR #29614 | After this PR | |---|---|---|---|---| | Individual | `proxy.individual` | Works | Works | Works | | Business | `proxy.business` | ❌ 421 | ✅ Works | ✅ Works + respects config | | Enterprise | `proxy.enterprise` | ❌ 421 | ✅ Works | ✅ Works + respects config | ## Related - Addresses review feedback from PR #29614 - Fixes: #41379, #8366, #17508 - Works with GPT-5.1-Codex, GPT-5.2-Codex, GPT-5.3-Codex, and other Copilot models **Tested on**: OpenClaw 2026.3.8, GitHub Copilot Business account ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T14:45:49Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds priority logic so that a user-configured `baseUrl` in `openclaw.json` takes precedence over the token-derived endpoint for GitHub Copilot Business/Enterprise accounts, building on the auto-detection work in PR #29614. The intent is correct, but there is a critical propagation bug: `effectiveModel` — the model object actually consumed by `runEmbeddedAttempt` — is computed as a shallow spread copy of `model` *before* `applyApiKeyInfo` is called. When context-window capping is active (a common scenario), writes to `model.baseUrl` are invisible to `effectiveModel`, meaning the token-derived `baseUrl` is never applied to the actual API call and the 421 fix would not function in those situations. Key points: - **Logic bug (both locations)**: `model.baseUrl` is mutated after `effectiveModel` is snapshotted; `effectiveModel.baseUrl` must be updated in parallel when `effectiveModel !== model`. - **Dead-code guard**: `copilotToken.baseUrl` is typed as `string` and is always non-empty, so the `copilotToken.baseUrl &&` guard in both sites is always true and should be removed for clarity.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge as-is: the core fix will silently do nothing when context-window capping is in effect, because `effectiveModel` (used for the actual API call) is a spread copy made before the baseUrl mutation. - The PR introduces a mutation to `model.baseUrl`, but `effectiveModel` — which is what `runEmbeddedAttempt` receives — is constructed as `{ ...model }` before `applyApiKeyInfo` is invoked whenever `ctxInfo.tokens < model.contextWindow`. In that spread path (common when a context cap is configured), the mutation is not reflected in the object the agent actually uses, so the Business/Enterprise endpoint fix does not take effect. This renders the primary goal of the PR unreliable without a fix to also update `effectiveModel.baseUrl`. - src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts — both added hunks need to also update `effectiveModel.baseUrl` when `effectiveModel !== model`.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts Line: 486-489 Comment: **`model.baseUrl` mutation not reflected in `effectiveModel`** `effectiveModel` is computed at line 388–391 — *before* `applyApiKeyInfo` is called — as a spread copy of `model` whenever `ctxInfo.tokens < model.contextWindow`: ```ts const effectiveModel = ctxInfo.tokens < (model.contextWindow ?? Infinity) ? { ...model, contextWindow: ctxInfo.tokens } // new object : model; // same reference ``` When a spread copy is created (the common case when a user has configured a context-token cap), `effectiveModel.baseUrl` is a snapshot of `model.baseUrl` taken at construction time. The subsequent mutations introduced by this PR (`model.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl`) write to the *source* object, but the *copy* passed to `runEmbeddedAttempt` at line 900 still holds the stale, pre-mutation value. As a result, the Copilot API call will target the wrong endpoint despite the token-derived URL being resolved correctly. The same problem applies to both the initial-auth path (this hunk) and the refresh path at line 632–635. To propagate the update correctly, `effectiveModel` must also be updated: ```ts if (copilotToken.baseUrl && (!model.baseUrl || model.baseUrl === DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL)) { model.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl; // Propagate to effectiveModel when it is a separate spread copy if (effectiveModel !== model) { effectiveModel.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl; } } ``` The same change is needed at the second site (line 632–635). How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts Line: 486-487 Comment: **`copilotToken.baseUrl` guard is always truthy** `resolveCopilotApiToken` declares its return type as `{ baseUrl: string; ... }` and always assigns either the token-derived URL or `DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL` as a fallback via the nullish-coalescing operator: ```ts baseUrl: deriveCopilotApiBaseUrlFromToken(payload.token) ?? DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL, ``` Therefore `copilotToken.baseUrl` is always a non-empty string, and the leading `copilotToken.baseUrl &&` check is dead code. It misleads readers into thinking the value could be falsy. Consider dropping it: ```suggestion if (!model.baseUrl || model.baseUrl === DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL) { ``` The identical redundant guard also appears at line 632. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #44294: Preserve structured ACP backend error kinds instead of mapping all errors to `end_turn`
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 44294
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/44294
- Title: Preserve structured ACP backend error kinds instead of mapping all errors to `end_turn`
- State: OPEN
- Author: aniruddhaadak80
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Problem

In `src/acp/translator.ts`, ACP events with `state === "error"` are currently finished as `end_turn` because the protocol lacks a structured error kind.

That means timeouts, rate limits, backend crashes, and deliberate refusals are all effectively collapsed into the same client-visible stop reason.

## Why It Matters

- Clients cannot distinguish transient backend failures from normal completion.
- Retry logic and UX become less accurate.
- Refusal semantics and operational failures are conflated.

## Suggested Direction

Introduce or consume a structured error classification such as:

- `refusal`
- `timeout`
- `rate_limit`
- `server_error`

Then map those cases intentionally instead of defaulting all ACP errors to `end_turn`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- ACP translator can preserve backend error class information.
- Clients can distinguish transient failures from normal end-of-turn behavior.
- The mapping is covered by targeted tests.

```

**Summary**
Keep open: current main still maps non-refusal Gateway ACP errorKind values such as timeout and rate_limit to ACP end_turn, and the remaining fix needs a maintainer decision on the ACP-visible error contract rather than a narrow code-only cleanup.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. A Gateway chat error with errorKind: "timeout" reaches the ACP translator and the current regression test asserts the prompt resolves with stopReason: "end_turn".

**Next step**
Needs maintainer API/product judgment on the ACP-visible error contract before a safe implementation can be scoped.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Define a documented additive ACP error-classification surface that carries Gateway errorKind or equivalent error codes alongside compatible StopReason values, then update translator/runtime schema docs and focused tests together.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, at source level. A Gateway chat error with errorKind: "timeout" reaches the ACP translator and the current regression test asserts the prompt resolves with stopReason: "end_turn".

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Unclear until maintainers choose the ACP-visible contract. Preserving backend error class information is the right direction, but inventing unsupported StopReason values is not the narrow maintainable fix.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: This is a normal-priority ACP error-signaling gap with clear source proof but limited blast radius and an API/product decision pending.
- `impact:session-state`: The affected value is the ACP prompt terminal stop reason, which clients use to interpret turn/session completion state.
- `impact:auth-provider`: The lost classifications include provider/backend failures such as rate limits and timeouts that influence retry and provider-handling behavior.

What I checked:

- **Current translator mapping:** Current main reads payload.errorKind for state === "error" but only maps "refusal" to ACP "refusal"; every other error kind resolves through finishPrompt as "end_turn". ([`src/acp/translator.ts:1423`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/3631af81076f/src/acp/translator.ts#L1423), [3631af81076f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/3631af81076f))
- **Focused source reproduction:** The current focused test sends a Gateway chat error event with errorKind: "timeout" and expects the ACP prompt response to be { stopReason: "end_turn" }. ([`src/acp/translator.error-kind.test.ts:26`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/3631af81076f/src/acp/translator.error-kind.test.ts#L26), [3631af81076f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/3631af81076f))
- **Gateway structured error kinds:** The Gateway chat protocol already defines structured errorKind values for refusal, timeout, rate_limit, context_length, and unknown on chat error events. ([`src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts:80`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/3631af81076f/src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts#L80), [3631af81076f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/3631af81076f))
- **Gateway emits classifications:** Gateway lifecycle handling reads or detects errorKind and includes it on chat error payloads; regression coverage asserts a 429-style error becomes errorKind: "rate_limit". ([`src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts:2113`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/3631af81076f/src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts#L2113), [3631af81076f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/3631af81076f))

- **Pinned ACP SDK StopReason contract:** The pinned @agentclientprotocol/sdk@0.21.1 StopReason union is end_turn, max_tokens, max_turn_requests, refusal, and cancelled, so timeout/rate_limit/context_length cannot be emitted as StopReason literals without a contract change. ([`package.json:1765`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/3631af81076f/package.json#L1765), [3631af81076f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/3631af81076f))

- **pejmanjohn:** Authored the merged change that moved ACP error states from unconditional refusal to end_turn before structured errorKind existed. (role: recent ACP translator contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [17c954c46e11](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/17c954c46e116dc271db70c418b3c963b4b9bfd9); files: `src/acp/translator.ts`, `src/acp/translator.session-rate-limit.test.ts`)

Remaining risk / open question:

- Adding timeout, rate_limit, or context_length directly as ACP StopReason literals would conflict with the pinned ACP SDK contract.
- The public ACP taxonomy is still undecided: Gateway currently has context_length and unknown, while the issue suggests server_error as an additional category.

Currently, these hooks only receive generic event data without session context: - `agent_end` event: only has `messages`, `success`, `error`, `durationMs` - `before_agent_start` event: only has `prompt`, `messages` This causes all memories to be tagged with `owner: agent:main` even when running as a different agent (e.g., 'qian-duoduo'). ## Solution Add `sessionKey` and `agentId` fields to both event types and populate them when triggering the hooks. ### Changes - `src/plugins/types.ts`: Add `sessionKey` and `agentId` to: - `PluginHookAgentEndEvent` - `PluginHookBeforeAgentStartEvent` - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`: Pass `sessionKey` and `agentId` when calling: - `runAgentEnd` - `runBeforeAgentStart` - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts`: Pass `sessionKey` and `agentId` when calling: - `runBeforeAgentStart` (legacy path) ## Related Issues - Complements PR #44011 which adds similar fields to message hooks - Follow-up to locked issue #24495, extending the same session/agent context propagation to hook event payloads - Fixes multi-agent memory isolation for memos-local and similar plugins ## Testing After this fix, memory plugins can now correctly identify the agent: ```javascript api.on("agent_end", async (event) => { console.log(event.sessionKey); // "agent:qian-duoduo:main" console.log(event.agentId); // "qian-duoduo" // Can now store memories with correct owner }); ``` ## Checklist - [x] Added type definitions for new fields - [x] Updated all call sites to pass the new data - [x] Maintains backward compatibility (fields are optional) - [x] Follows existing code style and conventions --- **Note**: This is a targeted fix for multi-agent memory isolation. github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44884 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44884 - Title: security: gateway public network hardening - State: OPEN - Author: yeyanle6 - Labels: gateway, commands, size: XL, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Implements comprehensive security hardening for OpenClaw Gateway when exposed to public networks (e.g. `https://openclaw.allegro.earth/`). The existing security mechanisms (non-loopback auth enforcement, auth rate limiting, CORS checks) leave gaps that this PR addresses: - **IP access control** (allowlist/blocklist with CIDR support) — enforced on both HTTP requests and WebSocket upgrades, loopback always allowed, blocklist takes priority - **Password/token strength validation** — CRITICAL startup warnings when network-exposed with short tokens (<32 chars) or weak passwords (<12 chars, all-digit/all-letter patterns) - **TLS enforcement check** — CRITICAL startup warning when network-exposed without TLS and no `terminatedUpstream` declaration - **Per-IP request rate limiting** — sliding window (default 120 req/min/IP), loopback exempt by default, returns 429 with Retry-After - **Auth audit logging** — JSONL to `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway-auth.jsonl` with file rotation, records `auth_failure`, `auth_success`, `rate_limited`, `ip_blocked` events - **Auto HSTS** — `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000` set automatically when TLS is enabled - **Eliminate silent 0.0.0.0 fallback** — `loopback`, `tailnet`, and `custom` bind modes now throw clear errors instead of silently falling back to all-interfaces - **New security audit checks** — `gateway.no_tls_network_exposed` (critical), `gateway.password_too_short` (critical), `gateway.password_weak_pattern` (warn), `gateway.no_request_rate_limit` (warn), `gateway.auto_bind_fallback` (warn) - **Enhanced `doctor` warnings** — TLS, password strength, token length, request rate limit checks for network-exposed gateways ### Config additions (`gateway.*`) | Key | Type | Default | Description | |-----|------|---------|-------------| | `ipAllowlist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP allowlist (loopback always allowed) | | `ipBlocklist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP blocklist (checked before allowlist) | | `requestRateLimit.maxRequests` | `number` | 120 | Max requests per IP per window | | `requestRateLimit.windowMs` | `number` | 60000 | Sliding window duration (ms) | | `requestRateLimit.exemptLoopback` | `boolean` | true | Skip rate limiting for localhost | | `tls.terminatedUpstream` | `boolean` | false | Declare TLS terminated by reverse proxy | ## Test plan - [x] `pnpm tsgo` — type check passes - [x] New unit tests: `ip-access-control.test.ts` (9 tests), `request-rate-limit.test.ts` (7 tests), `auth-audit-log.test.ts` (4 tests), `credential-strength.test.ts` (9 tests) - [x] Existing tests updated and passing: `server-runtime-config.test.ts`, `doctor-security.test.ts`, `net.test.ts`, `auth.test.ts`, `audit.test.ts` — 245 tests total - [ ] Manual: start gateway with `bind=lan` and verify startup warnings - [ ] Manual: test IP blocklist returns 403 - [ ] Manual: test request rate limit returns 429 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-13T08:54:10Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a comprehensive set of public-network security hardening features to the OpenClaw Gateway: IP allowlist/blocklist with CIDR support, per-IP request rate limiting, credential strength validation, TLS enforcement warnings, auth audit logging, auto-HSTS, and elimination of silent 0.0.0.0 bind fallbacks. The implementation is generally solid and well-tested, but there are a few gaps worth addressing: - **WebSocket IP blocks not audit-logged** — `attachGatewayUpgradeHandler` doesn't receive `authAuditLogger`, so blocked WebSocket upgrade attempts produce no `ip_blocked` audit entry, unlike HTTP requests. - **`authAuditLogger` not flushed on shutdown** — The shutdown sequence calls `requestRateLimiter.dispose()` but never `authAuditLogger.flush()`, risking loss of the last queued log entries on graceful stop. - **`auth_failure` / `auth_success` events declared but never emitted** — The type, the module docstring, and the PR description all state the logger records authentication outcomes, but no call sites for these events exist in this PR. - **Misleading `gateway.no_request_rate_limit` warning** — The rate limiter is always created with defaults (120 req/min/IP) regardless of explicit config. The doctor and audit findings that fire when no explicit `requestRateLimit` config is set claim rate limiting is absent, when it is actually active with defaults.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge with minor issues; the security hardening is effective but the audit log has gaps (WebSocket blocks unlogged, auth events unimplemented, no shutdown flush) and one misleading operator-facing warning. - Core security controls (IP ACL, rate limiting, credential checks, TLS warnings) are correctly implemented and tested. The gaps are in observability completeness (audit log) and messaging accuracy (misleading warning), not in the enforcement path itself. - `src/gateway/server-http.ts` (WebSocket audit logging gap), `src/gateway/server.impl.ts` (missing flush on shutdown), `src/gateway/auth-audit-log.ts` (unused event types), `src/security/audit.ts` (misleading no-rate-limit finding)

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `src/security/audit.ts`, line 1317-1327 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/e0f0938771125db417b4a0c983cab3a0cc8169ee/src/security/audit.ts#L1317-L1327)) **`gateway.no_request_rate_limit` finding is misleading — rate limiting is always active** `createRequestRateLimiter(cfgAtStart.gateway?.requestRateLimit)` is called unconditionally in `server.impl.ts` with defaults (`120 req/min/IP`, loopback exempt). Because `requestRateLimiter` is never `null`, the `if (requestRateLimiter)` guard in `server-http.ts` always fires, meaning **rate limiting is always active** even when no explicit config is present. The finding here fires when `!cfg.gateway?.requestRateLimit`, with the detail _"Without request rate limiting, the gateway is more vulnerable to abuse"_ — which is factually incorrect at runtime. The same problem exists in `doctor-security.ts` where the same condition triggers a _"No per-IP request rate limiting configured"_ warning. The message should clarify that defaults are applied automatically and the warning encourages explicit configuration, e.g.: > "gateway.requestRateLimit uses default limits (120 req/min/IP). Set it explicitly to acknowledge and tune this behavior."

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a 5-minute in-memory cache for `loadProviderUsageSummary` to prevent repeated OAuth usage API calls from triggering 429s during heartbeats and status polls. The implementation is clean overall — config schema, type definitions, help text, and labels are all well-coordinated — but two logic issues in the cache implementation need attention before merging. - **Clock inconsistency in `setCached`**: `setCached` computes `expiresAt` using `Date.now()` (wall clock), while `getCached` evaluates expiry against the caller-controlled `opts.now`. For any caller passing a future `opts.now`, the cache is immediately stale and never hit — defeating the purpose of caching in that scenario. - **Colliding empty-string cache key**: When `agentDir` is omitted, `cacheKey` returns `""` for all callers. Two callers using different `providers` lists but no `agentDir` will overwrite each other's entries and receive incorrect cached results. - The new tests validate cache hit and cache bypass on explicit `auth` correctly, though the cache-hit test passes somewhat accidentally because `usageNow` is a past timestamp (the real-time vs `opts.now` discrepancy in `setCached` happens to work for past values).

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge only after fixing the `setCached`/`now` clock inconsistency and the empty-string cache key collision. - The overall approach is sound and the config/schema changes are clean and backward-compatible. However, two logic bugs in `provider-usage.load.ts` could cause incorrect behavior: the `setCached` vs `getCached` clock mismatch breaks caching for future `opts.now` values, and the empty-string fallback for `cacheKey` allows cross-caller cache pollution when `agentDir` is not set. These should be resolved before the PR is merged. - src/infra/provider-usage.load.ts — `setCached` clock inconsistency (line 49) and empty-string cache key (line 31).

These two clocks can diverge. Concretely, if `opts.now` is a **future** timestamp (a fully valid scenario for predictive callers or time-travel tests), `getCached` will immediately see `futureNow >= Date.now() + ttlMs` as `true` on the very next call — the cache is never hit. The same applies in test environments where `opts.now` could be set to a controlled future timestamp. To keep the clocks consistent, `setCached` should accept and use the same `now` value: ```suggestion function setCached(key: string, value: UsageSummary, now: number, ttlMs: number): void { if (ttlMs <= 0) { return; } usageCache.set(key, { value, expiresAt: now + ttlMs }); } ``` And update the call site at line 151: ``` setCached(key, result, now, ttlMs); ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/infra/provider-usage.load.ts Line: 30-32 Comment: **Empty-string cache key collides across unrelated callers** When `agentDir` is not provided, `cacheKey` returns `""`. Any two callers that omit `agentDir` — but use different `providers` lists or entirely different configurations — will hit the same cache entry and receive each other's results. Consider including the resolved `providers` list in the key to prevent cross-caller collisions: ```suggestion function cacheKey(opts: UsageSummaryOptions): string { const providers = (opts.providers ?? usageProviders).slice().sort().join(","); return `${opts.agentDir ?? ""}|${providers}`; } ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

on a successful TTS call the chosen voice is written to prefs and used automatically on every subsequent reply until changed or cleared (empty string clears). - **Provider-agnostic by design**: the `voices` record is keyed by a plain string, so adding a new provider (e.g. MiniMax) requires no changes to this mechanism — only a one-line `getTtsVoice(prefsPath, "minimax")` read in the new provider's `textToSpeech` branch. ## Changes - `src/tts/tts.ts` — `TtsUserPrefs`, `TtsDirectiveParseResult`, `getTtsVoice`, `setTtsVoice`, `resolveVoiceOverrideForPersist`; confirm subsequent replies use `alloy` without the directive. - [ ] Send `[[tts:voiceId= persist=true]]` with ElevenLabs active; confirm voice persists across replies. - [ ] Pass empty string via `setTtsVoice` and confirm the persisted voice is cleared. 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-13T23:35:41Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a provider-agnostic voice-persistence mechanism to the TTS subsystem. The LLM can include `persist=true` in a `[[tts:...]]` directive; after a successful TTS call the chosen voice is written to the user prefs file (keyed by provider) and automatically applied to every subsequent call until changed or cleared. The implementation is clean and follows existing patterns (`getTtsProvider`/`setTtsProvider`), and the 12 new tests provide solid coverage. **Issues found:** - **Telephony path silently ignores persisted voice** (`tts.ts` line ~820): `textToSpeechTelephony` passes `config.elevenlabs.voiceId` / `config.openai.voice` directly to the TTS calls without consulting `getTtsVoice`. Users who persist a voice via the directive mechanism will not have it applied on the telephony path. The standard `textToSpeech` path was correctly updated; the telephony path was missed. - **`persist` token not gated by `allowVoice` policy** (`tts-core.ts` line 327): Every other voice-related directive key checks `if (!policy.allowVoice) { break; }` before taking action. The `persist` case sets the flag unconditionally. This is harmless today (no voice override enters `overrides` when `allowVoice` is false), but breaks the policy-checking invariant. - **`getTtsVoice` uses `||` instead of `??`** (`tts.ts` line 505): Could mishandle a manually-authored `""` entry in the JSON prefs file that `setTtsVoice` would otherwise prevent. - **`getTtsVoice`/`setTtsVoice` unnecessarily duplicated in `_test`** (`tts.ts` lines 1036-1037): Both are already public named exports; only private helpers like `resolveVoiceOverrideForPersist` need the `_test` escape hatch.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge after addressing the telephony path gap; the remaining issues are minor style inconsistencies. - The core feature is well-implemented with good test coverage and no data-corruption risk. The telephony path not reading the persisted voice is a genuine functional inconsistency that users relying on both standard and telephony TTS would notice — it's not a crash bug but it does leave the feature incomplete. The policy-check gap and `||`/`??` mismatch are low-severity style concerns. Together these warrant a 3/5. - `src/tts/tts.ts` — specifically `textToSpeechTelephony` (lines 814 and 846) which bypasses the persisted voice lookup.

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `src/tts/tts.ts`, line 820 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/709ec571fc7f357c7d55db6f6e660968a0a4b161/src/tts/tts.ts#L820)) **Persisted voice not applied in telephony path** `textToSpeechTelephony` (lines 780–867) calls `elevenLabsTTS` and `openaiTTS` using `config.elevenlabs.voiceId` / `config.openai.voice` directly, without consulting `getTtsVoice`. This means a voice persisted via `[[tts:voice=alloy persist=true]]` will be silently ignored on the telephony path, creating an inconsistency with the standard `textToSpeech` flow where the persisted voice is applied. If this path is intentionally excluded from persisted-voice reads, a comment to that effect would help future maintainers. Otherwise the fix mirrors what was done in `textToSpeech`: should become: ``` voiceId: getTtsVoice(prefsPath, "elevenlabs") ?? config.elevenlabs.voiceId, ``` and similarly for OpenAI voice in the telephony branch.

Also adds missing `prompt_tokens` and `completion_tokens` support to the CLI runner usage helper. Fixes #45038 Fixes #45061 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-13T23:45:21Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes token usage being reported as 0 for Alibaba Bailian and other OpenAI-compatible providers by replacing the `??`-chain field resolution in `normalizeUsage` (`usage.ts`) and `toUsage` (`cli-runner/helpers.ts`) with a two-pass `pick` helper that prefers the first positive value over any zero-valued field, then falls back to the first finite number if no positive value exists. **Key changes:** - Both files now share the same two-pass "prefer positive, fallback to first finite" strategy. Previously, `??` would stop at a zero value (e.g., `input_tokens: 0`), preventing a later positive value (`prompt_tokens: N`) from being used. - `helpers.ts` gains `prompt_tokens`/`promptTokens` for `input` and `completion_tokens`/`completionTokens` for `output`, making the CLI runner consistent with the main `usage.ts` field coverage. - `helpers.ts` also gains `cached_tokens` for `cacheRead`, matching `usage.ts`. **Correctness assessment:** The two-pass logic is sound across all edge cases: providers returning a single non-zero value behave identically to before; the new behavior only differs when at least one candidate field is 0 while another is positive. The existing negative-input clamp (`rawInput < 0 ? 0 : rawInput`) continues to function correctly in combination with the new `pick`. No unit tests were added covering the specific `input_tokens: 0, prompt_tokens: N` scenario; adding regression tests would be valuable to guard this fix going forward.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; the fix is logically correct and targeted, with no regressions for well-behaved providers. - The two-pass `pick` logic correctly resolves the reported zero-field bug and is consistent across both changed files. No critical logic errors or regressions were found. Score is 4 rather than 5 only because no regression tests were added for the specific `input_tokens: 0 / prompt_tokens: N` scenario that triggered the fix, leaving coverage of this edge case entirely to manual verification. - No files require special attention; both changes are self-contained and low-risk. GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 45739 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/45739 - Title: ACP: recover parent relay output from gateway state - State: OPEN - Author: stevenayl - Labels: agents, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - Problem: ACP child runs can finish successfully while the parent `streamTo: "parent"` relay only sees synthetic `start`/`stall` notices because it depends on local `onAgentEvent(...)` delivery. - Why it matters: orchestration from the parent session loses child progress and terminal completion, so `sessions_spawn` looks hung even when the ACP child actually replied or finished work. - What changed: the parent relay now backfills child assistant output from gateway `chat.history` and terminal state from `agent.wait`, while still preferring local assistant/lifecycle events when they arrive. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): ACP spawn semantics, child execution, and the normal local event path are unchanged; this only adds a best-effort fallback when relay events do not cross the process boundary. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #45205 - Related #45205 ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - ACP parent relays now recover missing child assistant output and completion from gateway state when local relay events do not arrive, so parent sessions stop getting stuck on `start`/`stall`-only updates. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - The relay now makes best-effort gateway calls to `chat.history` and `agent.wait` for the spawned child session/run. - Mitigation: calls are scoped to the known child session key / run ID, use short timeouts, and only supplement the existing local event path. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS host for source tests; live repro previously validated against Debian 13 install from issue pattern - Runtime/container: Node v25.6.0, pnpm 10.23.0 - Model/provider: ACP child via Codex during manual smoke; issue originally reported with Claude/OpenRouter - Integration/channel (if any): ACP `sessions_spawn` with `streamTo: "parent"` - Relevant config (redacted): ACP enabled with backend `acpx`, parent streaming enabled ### Steps 1. Start an ACP-capable parent session and spawn a child with `sessions_spawn`, `runtime: "acp"`, and `streamTo: "parent"`. 2. Use a task that produces visible assistant output quickly. 3. Observe the parent relay output and child transcript / ACP stream log. ### Expected - Parent relay receives child assistant progress and terminal completion. ### Actual - Before the fix, affected runs could emit only synthetic `start`/`stall` notices even though the child transcript already contained assistant output and the child had completed. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts src/commands/agent.acp.test.ts` - New relay regression cases cover: - missing local assistant + terminal events recovered via `chat.history` + `agent.wait` - missing local terminal event with assistant deltas still present - missing local assistant output recovered before a local lifecycle `end` - Manual live smoke on the installed 2026.3.12 runtime: 5 repeated real ACP spawns with unique markers all produced assistant output plus terminal completion in the generated `.acp-stream.jsonl` relay logs. - Edge cases checked: - start notice behavior unchanged - no-output stall / resume behavior unchanged - max relay lifetime timeout unchanged - local whitespace-preserving delta aggregation unchanged - What you did **not** verify: - I did not rely on `src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts` for this fix because that suite fully mocks `acp-spawn-parent-stream.js` and, in this tarball snapshot, currently fails during unrelated mock-hoisting/module-load setup before exercising the relay path. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: - Revert this commit or temporarily remove the fallback poll / gateway backfill from `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts`. - Files/config to restore: - `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts` - `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts` - `CHANGELOG.md` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - duplicated child progress relays - parent relay completion firing before child output is flushed - relay logs missing `assistant_history` / terminal backfill in the missing-event path ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: fallback history polling could duplicate output when local assistant events already arrived. - Mitigation: the fallback path bails once local assistant events have been seen and tracks whether history output was already relayed. - Risk: extra gateway lookups could introduce relay noise or hangs. - Mitigation: calls are short-timeout, best-effort, and capped to the relay lifetime / poll interval already used by the stall watcher. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-14T04:33:15Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a best-effort gateway fallback to the ACP parent stream relay so that child assistant output and terminal completion are recovered from `chat.history` and `agent.wait` when local `onAgentEvent` deliveries don't cross the process boundary. The approach is sound and the new tests cover the three key recovery scenarios. **Issues found:** - **Race condition in `primeHistoryBaseline`** (see inline comment): after the `async` gateway call resolves, the function does not re-check `historyBaselineReady`. A concurrent `maybeRelayHistoryAssistant` call from the first fallback poll can prime the baseline with the correct reply (setting `lastHistoryAssistant = "actual reply"`), then `primeHistoryBaseline` overwrites it with an earlier/empty result. This leaves the deduplication guard stale, causing the already-relayed output to be emitted a second time on the next poll cycle. A one-line `historyBaselineReady` guard after the `await` is the minimal fix.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Merge with caution — the fallback logic is largely correct but a missing post-await guard in primeHistoryBaseline can produce a duplicate output relay in a narrow race window. - The overall design is correct and the tests validate the main scenarios. The deduplication flags (historyOutputRelayed, sawLocalAssistantEvent, sawLocalTerminalEvent) provide layers of protection. However, primeHistoryBaseline does not re-check historyBaselineReady after its await, allowing a concurrent maybeRelayHistoryAssistant call to set valid state that primeHistoryBaseline then overwrites. The resulting stale lastHistoryAssistant baseline can cause a false "change" detection and a duplicate assistant output relay on the next poll tick. The impact is a redundant progress message (not a hung relay or data loss), but it's a real correctness issue in the new state machine. - src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts — specifically the primeHistoryBaseline function (lines 300-310)
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts Line: 300-310 Comment: **`primeHistoryBaseline` doesn't re-check `historyBaselineReady` after its `await`** `primeHistoryBaseline()` is fired with `void` at startup while the fallback poll timer starts at the same `fallbackPollIntervalMs`. Both paths can call `readLatestAssistantFromChildHistory()` concurrently (the priming call and the poll's `maybeRelayHistoryAssistant` call are not mutually guarded — `fallbackPollInFlight` only blocks concurrent poll runs, not the one-time `primeHistoryBaseline`). Race scenario: 1. `primeHistoryBaseline` starts: `historyBaselineReady = false`, gateway call in-flight. 2. The first fallback poll fires, `maybeRelayHistoryAssistant(false)` runs concurrently, also calls the gateway. Because `historyBaselineReady` is still `false`, it sets `historyBaselineReady = true`, `lastHistoryAssistant = "actual reply"`, and relays the output (`historyOutputRelayed = true`). 3. `primeHistoryBaseline` resolves: the `disposed` guard passes, but there is no check on `historyBaselineReady`. It unconditionally overwrites `lastHistoryAssistant` with whatever its own (potentially earlier/empty) gateway response returned — e.g. `lastHistoryAssistant = ""`. 4. On the next poll cycle: `reply = "actual reply"`, `lastHistoryAssistant = ""`, so `changed = true` and `historyOutputRelayed && !changed` evaluates to `false` — the output is emitted again. The fix is to add a `historyBaselineReady` guard after the `await`, mirroring the existing `disposed` check: ```suggestion const primeHistoryBaseline = async () => { if (historyBaselineReady || disposed) { return; } const reply = await readLatestAssistantFromChildHistory(); if (disposed || historyBaselineReady) { return; } historyBaselineReady = true; lastHistoryAssistant = reply ?? ""; }; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #46377: fix: add SSRF guard to Anthropic/Gemini PDF providers and move Gemini API key to header
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 46377 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/46377 - Title: fix: add SSRF guard to Anthropic/Gemini PDF providers and move Gemini API key to header - State: OPEN - Author: cdxiaodong - Labels: agents, size: S, triage: refactor-only, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - Both `anthropicAnalyzePdf()` and `geminiAnalyzePdf()` in `src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.ts` used raw `fetch()` with a user-controlled `baseUrl` parameter. An attacker could set `baseUrl` to an internal/private IP address, causing the server to make requests to internal services (SSRF). - The Anthropic function leaked the `x-api-key` header to any attacker-controlled destination. - The Gemini function passed the API key as a URL query parameter (`?key=...`), exposing it in server logs, proxy logs, and HTTP Referer headers (CWE-598). ## Changes - Replace raw `fetch()` with `fetchWithSsrFGuard(withStrictGuardedFetchMode(...))` in both functions, which validates the resolved hostname/IP against the SSRF blocklist before connecting. - For Gemini, move the API key from URL query parameter to the `x-goog-api-key` HTTP header to prevent credential leakage. - Add proper `release()` cleanup in `finally` blocks for both functions. ## Test plan - [ ] Verify Anthropic PDF analysis still works with default `https://api.anthropic.com` base URL - [ ] Verify Gemini PDF analysis still works with default `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com` base URL - [ ] Confirm that setting `baseUrl` to a private/internal IP (e.g. `http://169.254.169.254`) is blocked by the SSRF guard - [ ] Confirm Gemini API key no longer appears in URL query parameters ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-14T16:46:12Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes two concrete security vulnerabilities — SSRF exposure and API credential leakage — in `anthropicAnalyzePdf` and `geminiAnalyzePdf` by replacing raw `fetch()` calls with `fetchWithSsrFGuard(withStrictGuardedFetchMode(...))`, moving the Gemini API key from a URL query parameter to a request header, and adding proper `release()` cleanup in `finally` blocks. - **SSRF guard applied** — both functions now use `fetchWithSsrFGuard` with `STRICT` mode, which resolves the target hostname before connecting and rejects private/internal IPs, preventing a user-controlled `baseUrl` from reaching internal services. - **Gemini credential moved to header** — the API key is sent via `x-goog-api-key` instead of appending it to the URL, preventing exposure in server logs, proxy logs, and HTTP Referer headers (CWE-598). - **Credentials stripped on cross-origin redirects** — `fetchWithSsrFGuard` already strips non-safe headers on cross-origin redirects, so sensitive headers are not forwarded to unintended hosts. - **`release()` cleanup added** — both functions now call `release()` in a `finally` block, ensuring the pinned dispatcher is closed after every request outcome. - **Style note**: Neither call provides an `auditContext`, so blocked SSRF attempts will appear in logs as the generic `"url-fetch"` context rather than `"anthropic-pdf"` / `"gemini-pdf"`, reducing observability. The `try` blocks also open after the `await fetchWithSsrFGuard(...)` assignment rather than wrapping the full acquisition, which is functionally safe but unconventional.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge — the security fixes are correct and well-implemented, with only minor style improvements remaining. - The SSRF guard is applied correctly with strict mode, the Gemini API key leakage via query parameter is fixed, and `release()` cleanup is handled in `finally` blocks. The `fetchWithSsrFGuard` implementation also ensures credentials are stripped on cross-origin redirects. Minor issues: no `auditContext` set (reduces security log observability) and the `try` block doesn't cover the resource acquisition scope (safe today but unconventional). - No files require special attention beyond the two style suggestions noted above.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.ts Line: 68-86 Comment: **Consider adding `auditContext` for better SSRF log attribution** Neither provider specifies `auditContext` in the `fetchWithSsrFGuard` call. When an SSRF attempt is blocked, the guard logs a warning using the value of `auditContext ?? "url-fetch"`, so all blocked attempts from these functions will appear as the generic `"url-fetch"` context rather than something like `"anthropic-pdf"` or `"gemini-pdf"`. Adding it would make security incident investigations substantially easier. For `anthropicAnalyzePdf`: ```suggestion const { response: res, release } = await fetchWithSsrFGuard( withStrictGuardedFetchMode({ url: fetchUrl, auditContext: "anthropic-pdf", init: { ``` A similar change for `geminiAnalyzePdf` (line ~159–173) would set `auditContext: "gemini-pdf"`. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.ts Line: 87-118 Comment: **`try` block doesn't cover resource acquisition** The `try/finally` block opens *after* the `await fetchWithSsrFGuard(...)` call, meaning the `release` returned by a successful call is not guarded against an exception thrown between the awaited result assignment and the `try`. In practice this is safe today because `fetchWithSsrFGuard` calls its internal cleanup before re-throwing any error, making the returned `release` a no-op if it were skipped. However the pattern is fragile and may confuse future maintainers into thinking the `try` covers the full resource lifecycle. A more conventional pattern that makes the intent explicit: ```typescript let release: (() => Promise) | undefined; try { const result = await fetchWithSsrFGuard(withStrictGuardedFetchMode({ ... })); const res = result.response; release = result.release; // ... response handling return text.trim(); } finally { await release?.(); } ``` The same applies to the `geminiAnalyzePdf` function (lines 174–202). Without visibility into intermediate turns, the A2A feature cannot be used for user-facing expert collaboration. - **What changed:** Added a new `agent_to_agent_turn` plugin hook that fires after each completed turn of an A2A ping-pong exchange. Channel plugins can register handlers via `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", handler)` to intercept and forward these messages. - **What did NOT change:** No changes to A2A core logic, session management, or existing hook behavior. Configure two agents with `tools.agentToAgent.enabled: true` and mutual `allow` lists 2. Register a plugin handler: `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", (event) => console.log(event))` 3. Trigger an A2A exchange via `sessions_send` with `timeoutSeconds > 0` ### Expected - Handler is called after each ping-pong turn with `{ turn, speakerSessionKey, reply, ... }` - A2A exchange completes normally regardless of handler success/failure ### Actual - Verified via `pnpm build` — TypeScript compilation passes including exhaustiveness checks ## Evidence - [x] `pnpm build` passes — TypeScript exhaustiveness check (`MissingPluginHookNames extends never`) confirms all type registrations are consistent - [x] Hook follows exact same pattern as `subagent_spawned`/`subagent_ended` (void hook, `runVoidHook`, `hasHooks` guard) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: `pnpm build` compilation passes, exhaustiveness type check passes - Edge cases checked: hook handler errors are caught silently (cannot interrupt A2A exchange), `hasHooks` guard prevents overhead when no handlers registered - What I did **not** verify: End-to-end A2A exchange with a real channel plugin handler (requires running OpenClaw instance with DingTalk plugin) ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` — purely additive, no existing behavior changes - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the 3-file commit; or simply don't register the hook in plugins - Files/config to restore: `src/plugins/types.ts`, `src/plugins/hooks.ts`, `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts` - Known bad symptoms: If the hook runner import path is wrong, build will fail (caught by CI) ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Hook handler throws an error during A2A exchange - Mitigation: All hook calls are wrapped in `try/catch` — errors are silently swallowed, A2A exchange continues uninterrupted --- > **Disclosure:** This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude). All code has been reviewed and verified by @wjueyao. 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-14T23:55:16Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces a new `agent_to_agent_turn` plugin hook that fires after each completed turn in an A2A ping-pong exchange, enabling channel plugins (e.g. DingTalk, Discord) to forward intermediate expert-to-expert messages to users in real time. The change is purely additive — no existing behavior is altered — and the implementation correctly follows the patterns established by `subagent_spawned` / `subagent_ended`. - Three files changed: `types.ts` (type registration + exhaustiveness check), `hooks.ts` (`runAgentToAgentTurn` helper), and `sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts` (call site inside the ping-pong loop) - A redundant `typeof params.requesterChannel === "string"` check is present; since `GatewayMessageChannel` is always a string union, it can be simplified to `params.requesterChannel` directly - The outer `try/catch` around the hook call will never fire in practice because the global hook runner is configured with `catchErrors: true`, which already swallows per-handler errors internally - The initial target reply (`primaryReply`) that precedes the ping-pong loop is not emitted via this hook; plugin authors building full conversation-thread forwarding should be aware of this gap and may need to supplement via another hook (e.g. `message_sent`)

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; purely additive hook with no behavior change for existing flows and zero overhead when unused. - The implementation is clean and consistent with existing patterns. The exhaustiveness type check (`MissingPluginHookNames extends never`) provides strong compile-time assurance of correctness. The only notable concerns are two minor style redundancies and a potential documentation gap around the initial reply not being emitted — none of which are blocking. - No files require special attention; `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts` is worth a second glance for the initial-reply coverage gap, but it does not represent a blocking issue.

For the stated use case — "channel plugins (e.g. DingTalk) need to see the discussion process between experts" — this means the very first message in the expert-to-expert exchange is invisible to plugin handlers. Depending on whether `primaryReply` is already surfaced through another hook (e.g. `message_sent`), this may leave a gap in the conversation thread forwarded to users. Worth explicitly documenting or considering whether turn 0 / the initial reply also needs to be emitted.

target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #46740: ACP: classify silent acpx exits as backend unavailable
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 46740 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/46740 - Title: ACP: classify silent acpx exits as backend unavailable - State: OPEN - Author: clovericbot - Labels: size: S, extensions: acpx, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - classify silent non-zero acpx exits as `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` instead of generic `ACP_TURN_FAILED` - preserve the existing permission-denied guidance while improving the fallback used when the runtime backend exits without a structured ACP error - cover the runtime, manager, and user-facing error text regressions for `acpx exited with code 1` ## Testing - pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/acp/runtime/error-text.test.ts ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T02:23:08Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR reclassifies silent (no stderr) non-zero exits from the `acpx` backend as `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` rather than the generic `ACP_TURN_FAILED`, giving users more actionable guidance (`/acp doctor`) when the backend process disappears without a structured error. Permission-denied exits (code 5) are deliberately kept as `ACP_TURN_FAILED` to preserve the existing configuration guidance. The change is applied in two layers: - **`extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`** – `resolveSilentAcpxExitErrorCode` is called directly when the ACP runtime detects a silent exit in `runTurn` (streaming path) and in the private `runControlCommand` helper (control-command path). - **`src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts`** – A defensive regex-based fallback (`resolveTurnFailureFallbackCode`) catches the rare case where the runtime emits a plain `Error` (not `AcpRuntimeError`) with the `"acpx exited with code N"` message text. Key observations: - The `runControlCommand` change is broader than the PR description implies: it also affects session-init command paths that previously used `fallbackCode: "ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED"`. A silent exit during `ensureSession` (e.g. `sessions new`) will now surface as `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` instead of `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, changing both user-facing guidance and the Discord monitor's session-staleness classification (`classifyAcpStatusProbeError` in `src/discord/monitor/provider.ts` has an explicit branch for `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`). This may be intentional and arguably more correct, but it is undocumented. - The regex in `manager.core.ts` creates a soft coupling to the exact string produced by `formatAcpxExitMessage`; both are stable today, but the dependency is implicit. - Test coverage for the primary `runTurn` path (runtime test), the manager's fallback path (manager test), and the error-text routing (error-text test) is solid.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge with minor follow-up: the session-init reclassification side-effect should be acknowledged and ideally tested. - The core logic is correct and well-tested across three test files. No critical bugs found. The main concern is an undocumented behavioral change: `runControlCommand` now returns `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` for session-init silent exits (previously `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`), which has downstream effects on user guidance text and the Discord monitor's session classification. This is arguably better behavior but it is not mentioned in the PR description and lacks a dedicated test to lock in the expectation. - extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts (runControlCommand silent-exit path) and src/discord/monitor/provider.ts (classifyAcpStatusProbeError ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED branch)

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`, line 812-820 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/c50968f53cdd9787a3cd0dcbc8b74d75d3b935b2/extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts#L812-L820)) **Silent-exit reclassification also affects session-init callers** `runControlCommand` is a shared private method called both for turn operations (`fallbackCode: "ACP_TURN_FAILED"`) and for session initialisation (`fallbackCode: "ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED"`). By replacing `params.fallbackCode` with `resolveSilentAcpxExitErrorCode` at the silent-exit branch, a silent non-zero exit during session initialisation (e.g. `ensureSession` → `sessions new`) now throws `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` instead of `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`. Concretely, two things change for that path: 1. **User-facing guidance** – `formatAcpRuntimeErrorText` routes `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` to "If this session is stale, recreate it with `/acp spawn`…" and routes `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` to "Run `/acp doctor`, install/enable the backend plugin, then retry." The latter guidance is arguably more appropriate for a backend that silently crashed, but it is a visible UX change not mentioned in the PR description. 2. **Discord monitor session classification** – `classifyAcpStatusProbeError` (`src/discord/monitor/provider.ts:167`) has an explicit branch for `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` that marks the binding as `"stale"`. With the new code that path is bypassed, and the fallback becomes `"uncertain"` (unless `isStaleRunning` is set), which could affect binding-teardown behaviour. If this broadening is intentional, it is worth documenting in the PR description and adding a test for the `ensureSession` silent-exit path (analogous to the new `runtime.test.ts` test) to lock in the expected code.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 46949 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/46949 - Title: fix(acp): release dormant oneshot runtimes under pressure - State: OPEN - Author: lanyasheng - Labels: size: L, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - release cached oneshot runtime handles after startup identity reconciliation - evict dormant oneshot ACP runtimes before stale-status probing under concurrent session pressure - add regression coverage for pending-identity oneshot sessions and pressure eviction of idle oneshot handles ## Problem OpenClaw could report `ACP max concurrent sessions reached (13/12)` even when ACPX session registry and process state looked clean. In our repro, dormant oneshot sessions could remain counted inside the ACP manager runtime cache, especially after startup identity reconciliation or quick oneshot completion. ## Root cause The ACP session manager only reclaimed obviously stale runtimes under pressure. Oneshoot handles that were no longer actively running could still remain cached and count toward `acp.maxConcurrentSessions`. ## Fix 1. During `reconcilePendingSessionIdentities()`, if the reconciled session is `mode: "oneshot"`, always `runtime.close()` it and clear the cached handle. 2. Before the existing stale-runtime probe path, evict cached oneshot handles whose persisted ACP metadata is no longer `running`. 3. Keep the existing stale probing as a fallback for genuinely stale persistent runtimes. ## Tests - `pnpm exec vitest run src/acp/control-plane/*.test.ts` - 34 passed ## Notes This patch addresses the manager-side leak/counting path. I observed separate downstream task-completion heuristics in a local `spawn-interceptor` plugin, but those are outside the OpenClaw core change in this PR. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T06:03:37Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a back-pressure eviction mechanism to `AcpSessionManager` so that when all concurrent session slots are full, the manager probes idle sessions for liveness (via `getStatus` with a 5-second timeout) and evicts stale ones before rejecting the new session request, preventing zombie sessions from permanently blocking new session creation. Key observations: - **Race condition in `evictStaleRuntimesUnderPressure`**: The `activeTurnBySession` guard is evaluated at snapshot time, but the `getStatus` probe is then awaited for up to 5 seconds. A new turn can arrive for the candidate session during this window, start executing, and set `activeTurnBySession` — but the eviction code already passed the guard and will still call `runtimeCache.clear()` + `runtime.close()` on the active session. The existing `evictIdleRuntimeHandles` avoids this by wrapping each eviction inside `this.actorQueue.run(candidate.actorKey, ...)` so the check is atomic with the eviction; the new method does not apply the same pattern. - **Eviction stats not updated**: Back-pressure evictions do not increment `this.evictedRuntimeCount` or update `this.lastEvictedAt`, making them invisible to any monitoring code that reads these fields. - The test suite covers the four happy/sad paths described in the PR but does not include a test for the race window (a turn starting during a `getStatus` probe).

Confidence Score: 3/5

- The change has a real race condition that can call `runtime.close()` on a session actively running a turn; needs the same `actorQueue.run(candidateKey)` guard used by `evictIdleRuntimeHandles`. - The overall design and test coverage for the intended scenarios are solid, but the missing actor-queue guard in `evictStaleRuntimesUnderPressure` is a correctness issue — the active-turn check is evaluated before a potentially 5-second async operation, creating a window where a live session's process could be terminated mid-turn. - src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts — specifically the `evictStaleRuntimesUnderPressure` method
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts Line: 1173-1211 Comment: **Race condition: active-turn check not re-validated after async `getStatus` probe** The candidate list is filtered with `!this.activeTurnBySession.has(entry.actorKey)` at snapshot time (line 1173). A `getStatus` probe then runs for **up to 5 seconds** (line 1185). If a new turn arrives for `candidate.actorKey` during that window — it passes the already-evaluated filter, enters `withSessionActor`, and sets `activeTurnBySession` — the code below will still proceed to `this.runtimeCache.clear(candidate.actorKey)` and then call `runtime.close(...)` on a session that is actively running a turn. Compare with `evictIdleRuntimeHandles` (line 1129), which correctly re-checks `activeTurnBySession` **inside** `this.actorQueue.run(candidate.actorKey, ...)`, serialising the liveness check with any concurrent turn: ```typescript await this.actorQueue.run(candidate.actorKey, async () => { if (this.activeTurnBySession.has(candidate.actorKey)) { return; // turn started between snapshot and now — skip } // safe to evict }); ``` The same guard should be applied here. After a successful eviction is decided (i.e., `isStale === true`), the actual `runtimeCache.clear` + `runtime.close` calls should be wrapped in (or preceded by an inline re-check within) `this.actorQueue.run(candidate.actorKey, ...)` to ensure no turn has started during the async probe window. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts Line: 1199-1212 Comment: **Eviction stats not updated for back-pressure evictions** `evictIdleRuntimeHandles` increments `this.evictedRuntimeCount` and updates `this.lastEvictedAt` whenever a session is cleared (lines 1142-1143). `evictStaleRuntimesUnderPressure` skips both updates, so back-pressure evictions are invisible to any monitoring/reporting code that reads these fields. ```typescript this.runtimeCache.clear(candidate.actorKey); this.evictedRuntimeCount += 1; // add these two lines this.lastEvictedAt = Date.now(); // to keep stats consistent reclaimed += 1; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````
Last reviewed commit: 1a05ae9 - lanyasheng at 2026-03-15T06:17:29Z: Thanks for the thorough review! Both issues are valid and have been addressed in commit f654f7e: **1. Race condition fix**: Wrapped the entire eviction logic (getStatus probe + clear + close) inside `this.actorQueue.run(candidate.actorKey, ...)`, matching the pattern used by `evictIdleRuntimeHandles`. Also added a second `activeTurnBySession` check after the async `getStatus` probe to guard against turns that started during the 5-second probe window. **2. Eviction stats fix**: Added `this.evictedRuntimeCount += 1` and `this.lastEvictedAt = Date.now()` after back-pressure evictions so they are visible to monitoring/reporting code. - clawsweeper at 2026-04-28T07:07:03Z: Codex review: needs real behavior proof before merge. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.
