GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 47083 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/47083 - Title: fix: respect totalTokensFresh flag to avoid showing stale token counts - State: OPEN - Author: zymclaw - Labels: app: web-ui, size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem When a session's `totalTokensFresh` flag is `false`, the `totalTokens` value may be stale/historical (e.g., accumulated from previous compaction) and should not be displayed. This caused incorrect displays like "100% context used 2.6M / 200k" in both TUI and Web UI when the token count was stale. ## Root Cause The Gateway already correctly filters stale token counts via `resolveFreshSessionTotalTokens`, but: 1. **TUI**: Type definition was missing `totalTokensFresh` field, so TUI couldn't check it 2. **UI**: `formatSessionTokens` function didn't check the freshness flag ## Solution ### TUI (commit 368ca03f1) - Add `totalTokensFresh` to `GatewaySessionList` type in `gateway-chat.ts` - Add `totalTokensFresh` to `SessionInfoEntry` type in `tui-session-actions.ts` - Check `totalTokensFresh !== false` before updating `sessionInfo.totalTokens` ### UI (commit 446fdd13a) - Add `totalTokensFresh` to `GatewaySessionRow` type in `types.ts` - Check `totalTokensFresh === false` in `formatSessionTokens` function, return "n/a" when stale ## Testing - TypeScript compilation passes for both TUI and UI - The fix ensures stale token counts are not displayed in sessions list ## Files Changed - `src/tui/gateway-chat.ts` - Add `totalTokensFresh` to type definition - `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts` - Add type and check before using `totalTokens` - `ui/src/ui/types.ts` - Add `totalTokensFresh` to type definition - `ui/src/ui/presenter.ts` - Check freshness before displaying tokens ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T08:22:22Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a TUI bug where stale `totalTokens` values (marked with `totalTokensFresh: false` by the Gateway, e.g., after session compaction) were incorrectly applied and displayed in the footer. The fix correctly adds `totalTokensFresh` to the relevant type definitions and gates the `totalTokens` update behind a freshness check. **Key changes:** - `gateway-chat.ts`: Adds `totalTokensFresh?: boolean` to `GatewaySessionList` — a clean, additive type change. - `tui-session-actions.ts`: Extends `SessionInfoEntry` with `totalTokensFresh` and checks the flag before updating `totalTokens` in `applySessionInfo`. **One issue to address:** The new outer guard `entry.totalTokens !== null` is evaluated before the freshness check. Because `totalTokens` is typed as `number | null`, this inadvertently prevents a fresh `null` value from clearing an existing stale count in state (see inline comment). Removing the null guard from the outer condition and relying solely on the inner freshness check handles all four combinations correctly.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge with a minor fix — the freshness check is correct, but the placement of the `null` guard introduces a subtle regression for fresh-null resets. - The core logic (don't apply `totalTokens` when `totalTokensFresh === false`) is sound and addresses the stated bug. However, the `entry.totalTokens !== null` check placed in the outer condition silently drops `null` even when `totalTokensFresh` is `true`, which changes the pre-existing behaviour of clearing the token count. This is a real edge-case regression worth fixing before merge. - src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts — lines 194–198, the null guard ordering
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts Line: 194-198 Comment: **`null` guard blocks legitimate fresh-null resets** `totalTokens` is typed as `number | null` in `SessionInfo`. When the gateway sends `totalTokens: null` (to clear/reset the displayed count) together with `totalTokensFresh: true` (or `totalTokensFresh` absent), the outer `entry.totalTokens !== null` guard silently drops the update — leaving a stale numeric value on-screen. The `null` check is needed only when the value is stale, but currently it runs unconditionally before the freshness check is ever evaluated. Restructuring the condition handles all four combinations correctly: ```suggestion if (entry?.totalTokens !== undefined) { if (entry.totalTokensFresh !== false) { next.totalTokens = entry.totalTokens; } } ``` This way: - `totalTokens: 5000, fresh: true` → updated ✓ - `totalTokens: 5000, fresh: false` → skipped (stale) ✓ - `totalTokens: null, fresh: true` → clears display ✓ - `totalTokens: null, fresh: false` → skipped (stale null) ✓ How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

Users on the QMD backend will see no isolation even when `memory.isolation.enabled` is `true`. - **`--last` CLI flag is a no-op** (`src/cli/config-cli.ts:538`): The `--last` option is defined, documented, and passed into `runConfigRestore`, but `opts.last` is never read in the function body. Greptile Summary

This PR adds native Azure OpenAI support to the `memory-lancedb` plugin's `Embeddings` class by detecting Azure endpoints (via `.openai.azure.com` in `baseUrl`) and automatically injecting the `api-key` header and `api-version` query parameter required by Azure OpenAI's REST API. This is a clean, minimal change that avoids requiring a proxy such as LiteLLM. - The detection heuristic (`baseUrl?.includes(".openai.azure.com")`) is reliable for all standard Azure OpenAI resource URLs. - The `api-key` header injection is the correct mechanism for Azure authentication through the OpenAI SDK. - **The `api-version` is hardcoded to `"2024-02-01"`** — one of the earlier GA releases — with no way for users to override it via config. `config.ts`'s `assertAllowedKeys` does not accept an `apiVersion` field, so users on tenants that deprecate `2024-02-01`, or those needing features from a newer API version, currently have no path to configure this.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 47446 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/47446 - Title: fix(gateway/discord): respect env proxy vars and prevent ECONNRESET - State: OPEN - Author: winter-loo - Labels: channel: discord, gateway, cli, size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - Bootstrap global `EnvHttpProxyAgent` in gateway startup so Node's `fetch()` honors `https_proxy`/`HTTP_PROXY` env vars — fixes Discord REST calls (command deployment, bot identity fetch) timing out behind local proxies - Discord gateway plugin now falls back to env proxy vars for WebSocket connections when no explicit `channels.discord.proxy` is configured - Disable `keepAlive` on all proxy `ProxyAgent` instances to prevent `ECONNRESET` from local proxies (Clash, Surge, etc.) that close HTTP CONNECT tunnels after one request ## Test plan - [x] `pnpm test -- src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts` passes (10/10) - [x] `pnpm test -- extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.rest-proxy.test.ts` passes (8/8) - [ ] Manual: verify Discord gateway connects and responds to interactions behind a local proxy with `https_proxy` set 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T15:55:24Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes two proxy-related reliability issues for Discord gateway connections: it bootstraps a global `EnvHttpProxyAgent` so Node's `fetch()` honours `https_proxy`/`HTTP_PROXY` env vars, and disables `keepAlive` on all `ProxyAgent` / `EnvHttpProxyAgent` instances to prevent `ECONNRESET` from local proxies (Clash, Surge, etc.) that close HTTP CONNECT tunnels after a single request. - `run.ts`: adds `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher()` before `ensureGlobalUndiciStreamTimeouts()` so that the timeout options wrap the already-active `EnvHttpProxyAgent` (ordering comment is clear and correct). - `undici-global-dispatcher.ts`: `keepAlive: false` is added to both the initial proxy bootstrap and the timeout-wrapped `EnvHttpProxyAgent`. Minor: the spread on line 126 is `{ keepAlive: false, ...connect }` — if `resolveConnectOptions` were ever extended with a `keepAlive` property, it would silently override the intent; `{ ...connect, keepAlive: false }` would be safer. - `gateway-plugin.ts`: falls back to `resolveEnvHttpProxyUrl("https")` for WebSocket and gateway REST fetches when no explicit `channels.discord.proxy` is set. Per-request `dispatcher` overrides the global `EnvHttpProxyAgent`, so there is no double-proxy routing. - `rest-fetch.ts`: `ProxyAgent` constructor updated to pass `{ uri, connect: { keepAlive: false } }`. - Test files: mocks updated to accept the new constructor signature; `resolveEnvHttpProxyUrl` is mocked to `undefined` in existing gateway tests to isolate them from the env fallback.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; one minor defensive-coding concern with spread order that poses no current risk. - The changes are focused, well-tested (10 + 8 unit tests), and the ordering of bootstrap calls is correct. The only concern is the `{ keepAlive: false, ...connect }` spread order in `ensureGlobalUndiciStreamTimeouts` — because `connect` currently never includes `keepAlive`, there is no active bug, but the ordering is fragile if `resolveConnectOptions` is ever extended. - `src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.ts` line 126 — spread order of `proxyConnect`.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.ts Line: 126 Comment: **Spread order may allow `connect` to override `keepAlive: false`** The intent of `proxyConnect` is to always enforce `keepAlive: false` to prevent ECONNRESET from local proxies. However, using `{ keepAlive: false, ...connect }` means any `keepAlive` property that `connect` carries (e.g., if `resolveConnectOptions` is ever extended) would override the `false` value. For the invariant to hold unconditionally, `keepAlive: false` should be the last spread: ```suggestion const proxyConnect = { ...connect, keepAlive: false }; ``` Currently `resolveConnectOptions` never returns `keepAlive`, so there is no active bug — but the defensive order guards against future regressions. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 47479 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/47479 - Title: docker: fix docker-setup.sh .env handling - State: OPEN - Author: tkothe - Labels: docker, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem Two related issues with `docker-setup.sh` and `.env` handling: ### 1. `docker-setup.sh` does not source `.env` Variables defined in `.env` (e.g. `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER`) were silently ignored unless the caller manually ran `source .env` before invoking `docker-setup.sh`. This is a common footgun — the file exists and looks authoritative, but has no effect on the build. ### 2. `upsert_env()` strips quotes from whitespace-containing values `upsert_env()` rewrites `.env` using bare `printf '%s=%s\n'`, which drops quotes. After a `docker-setup.sh` run, a value like: ``` OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES="jq ripgrep" ``` becomes: ``` OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES=jq ripgrep ``` On the next invocation, `source .env` fails with `command not found: ripgrep`, silently breaking multi-package build args. ## Fix - **Source `.env` at startup** using `set -a / source / set +a` so all `.env` variables are exported before the script proceeds. - **Add `_write_kv()` helper** in `upsert_env()` that wraps values containing spaces or tabs in double quotes when writing back to `.env`. ## Testing ```bash echo 'OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES="jq ripgrep"' >> .env echo 'OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER=1' >> .env ./docker-setup.sh grep -E 'APT_PACKAGES|INSTALL_BROWSER' .env # Expected: values preserved with quotes intact ``` ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T16:37:54Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes two bugs in `docker-setup.sh`: `.env` variables were silently ignored unless the caller manually sourced the file, and `upsert_env()` stripped quotes from multi-word values on write-back, breaking subsequent invocations. The fix introduces a safe, manual `.env` parser at startup (avoiding `source`-based shell injection) and a `_write_kv()` helper that preserves quoting on rewrite. All previously-raised review concerns — identifier validation, backslash escaping order, comment stripping for unquoted values only, double-quote-only values triggering the quoting branch, and environment precedence (caller env wins over `.env`) — have been addressed in this revision. - The new parser correctly skips already-exported variables via `${!_env_key+x}`, preserving caller-env-over-file precedence. - `_write_kv` escapes backslashes before double-quotes, and the reader reverses in the same order (unescape `\"` first, then `\\`), producing a correct round-trip. - Inline-comment stripping is gated on the value being unquoted, protecting fully-quoted values from truncation. - Identifier validation prevents crashes on lines prefixed with `export`, hyphens, or other invalid bash identifier characters.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; the two targeted bugs are correctly fixed and all previously raised review concerns are addressed in this revision. - The custom `.env` parser is non-trivial but the implementation correctly handles quoting, escaping, comment stripping, identifier validation, and env-var precedence. The round-trip between `_write_kv` (writer) and the startup parser (reader) was verified to be consistent for all relevant value shapes (backslash-only, quote-only, backslash+quote, empty). No new logical issues were found beyond what the previous review threads already captured and that have now been resolved. One point is deducted for the inherent complexity of a hand-rolled `.env` parser — exotic user-authored `.env` syntax (e.g. multi-line values, heredoc syntax) is not supported, but this is out of scope for this script. - No files require special attention. Body:
```markdown
# [Feature Request] Add tool call visibility to streamTo="parent" for ACP runtime

## Current Behavior

When using `runtime="acp"` with `streamTo="parent"`, only the following events are streamed back to the parent session:

1. **Assistant text output** - Text deltas from the child agent
2. **Lifecycle events** - Start, end, error states

**What's missing:**
- Tool calls (which tools are being invoked)
- Tool call arguments (what parameters are passed)
- Tool results (what the tools returned)
- Thinking process (if enabled)

This makes the child agent a "black box" - you can see what it says, but not what it's doing.

## Desired Behavior

When `streamTo="parent"` is enabled, the parent session should receive progress updates including:

- 🔧 **Tool calls**: `"🔧 Calling tool: exec (command: 'ls -la')"`
- ✅ **Tool results**: `"✅ Tool result: [truncated output]"`
- 💭 **Thinking**: `"💭 Thinking: [summary]"` (if thinking is enabled)

This provides full transparency into what the child agent is doing.

## Implementation Suggestion

Modify `startAcpSpawnParentStreamRelay()` in `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts` to subscribe to additional event types:

```javascript
// Current implementation only handles:
if (event.stream === "assistant") { ... }
if (event.stream === "lifecycle") { ... }

// Add support for:
if (event.stream === "tool_call") {
    emit(`🔧 ${event.data.name}(${summarizeArgs(event.data.arguments)})`, ...);
}
if (event.stream === "tool_result") {
    emit(`✅ Result: ${truncate(event.data.content, 200)}`, ...);
}
if (event.stream === "thinking") {
    emit(`💭 ${truncate(event.data.thinking, 150)}`, ...);
}
```

## Verbose Control

**Question for maintainers:** Should this behavior respect the parent session's verbose setting?

- Option A: Always show tool calls when `streamTo="parent"` is set
- Option B: Inherit parent session's `/v` (verbose) setting
- Option C: Add a new parameter like `streamVerbose: "full" | "minimal" | "inherit"`

I personally prefer **Option B** (inherit verbose setting) for consistency with existing UX.

## Use Cases

- Debugging child agent behavior in real-time
- Understanding why a task is taking long (e.g., waiting for a slow API call)
- Monitoring resource usage (e.g., seeing which files are being read)
- Educational purposes (learning how agents work)

## Related

This is a prerequisite for #[TBD] (Add streamTo support for runtime="subagent")

```

This PR adds configurable `reactionSemantics` to the Telegram channel, allowing operators to map standard emoji and Telegram `custom_emoji` reactions to named product-level meanings (`wake`, `queue`, or `ignore`). The core logic is cleanly extracted into a new `reaction-events.ts` module with solid normalisation and semantic resolution helpers, and the `bot-handlers.ts` reaction path is refactored to use them. Greptile Summary

This PR adds image attachment support to the TUI via two mechanisms: an `@path` inline syntax for embedding images in messages, and a `/image ` slash command. A new `tui-image-extract.ts` module handles path extraction and file loading, with solid unit-test coverage for both happy paths and error cases. **Key findings:** - **Logic bug** (`tui-command-handlers.ts:565`): When the entire message is an image reference (e.g. `@./screenshot.png` with no other text), `extractImagePaths` returns `cleanText: ""`. The `cleanText || text` fallback then sends the raw `@`-path string to the gateway and shows it in the chat log instead of a useful placeholder. - **TOCTOU race** (`tui-image-extract.ts:88–101`): File size is checked with `fs.stat` then the file is read with a separate `fs.readFile` call. Reading the buffer first and checking `buffer.byteLength` eliminates the race and reduces syscalls. - **Incorrect ESLint disable** (`tui-image-extract.ts:29`): The `// eslint-disable-next-line no-control-regex` comment does not apply to the regex below it (no control characters are used); it should be removed.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Not safe to merge as-is — one logic bug causes the raw `@path` syntax to be sent to the gateway when no other text accompanies the image reference. - The feature is well-structured and has good test coverage, but the `cleanText || text` fallback in `sendMessage` is a real logic bug that will produce confusing UX and wrong message content for a common use case. The other two issues (TOCTOU and lint comment) are minor. Fixing the message fallback is needed before merging. - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.ts` (line 565) requires the `cleanText || text` fallback fix; `src/tui/tui-image-extract.ts` has a minor TOCTOU and an incorrect ESLint suppression.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/tui/tui-command-handlers.ts Line: 565 Comment: **`@path`-only message sends raw path syntax to gateway** When the entire message is an image reference (e.g. the user types `@./screenshot.png` with no accompanying text), `extractImagePaths` returns `cleanText: ""`. The `||` fallback then sets `message = text`, which is the original raw string `@./screenshot.png`. This raw `@`-reference is then shown in the chat log via `chatLog.addUser(message)` and sent to the gateway, instead of an empty string or a meaningful placeholder. ```suggestion message = cleanText !== "" ? cleanText : "[image attached]"; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/tui/tui-image-extract.ts Line: 88-101 Comment: **TOCTOU race between `stat` and `readFile`** The file's size is checked via `fs.stat`, but the file is read separately with `fs.readFile`. In the window between the two calls the file could be replaced or appended to, so a file that passes the 5 MB guard could ultimately be read at a larger size. Reading the buffer first and then checking `buffer.byteLength` eliminates the race and avoids an extra syscall: ```typescript let buffer: Buffer; try { buffer = await fs.readFile(resolved); } catch { throw new Error(`file not found: ${rawPath}`); } if (buffer.byteLength > MAX_IMAGE_BYTES) { throw new Error( `file too large: ${rawPath} (${buffer.byteLength} bytes, max ${MAX_IMAGE_BYTES} bytes)`, ); } const content = buffer.toString("base64"); ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/tui/tui-image-extract.ts Line: 29 Comment: **Incorrect ESLint disable comment** `no-control-regex` flags regex patterns that match Unicode control characters (code points U+0000–U+001F). The `IMAGE_REF_RE` regex contains no such patterns — it only uses ordinary escape sequences like `\\.` and `\\s`. The suppression comment is unnecessary and misleading; it should be removed to avoid masking a real lint warning in the future. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

memory_search tool returns "fetch failed" error despite embedding provider correctly configured

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure embedding provider in openclaw.json:
   {
     "bailian-embedding": {
       "baseUrl": "https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1",
       "apiKey": "sk-***",
       "api": "openai-completions",
       "models": [{"id": "text-embedding-v3", "name": "text-embedding-v3"}]
     }
   }

2. Restart Gateway: openclaw gateway restart

3. Call memory_search tool with any query

4. Observe error: "fetch failed" / "embedding/provider error"

### Expected behavior

memory_search should:
1. Use the configured embedding provider (bailian-embedding)
2. Generate embeddings for the query text
3. Search memory files by semantic similarity
4. Return relevant memory results

### Actual behavior

memory_search returns error immediately:
```json
{
  "results": [],
  "disabled": true,
  "unavailable": true,
  "error": "fetch failed",
  "warning": "Memory search is unavailable due to an embedding/provider error."
}

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.2

### Operating system

macOS 21.6.0 (Darwin x64)

### Install method

npm global install

### Model

Primary: bailian/qwen3.5-plus Embedding: text-embedding-v3 (Aliyun Bailian)

### Provider / routing chain

Provider: bailian (Aliyun DashScope) Base URL: https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1 Embedding Provider: bailian-embedding Embedding Base URL: https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1

### Config file / key location

openclaw.json

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell

```

### Impact and severity

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Ryce at 2026-03-16T05:13:00Z:
This looks like credential/URL drift between what's in openclaw.json and what the gateway actually resolves at runtime. Worth checking:

1. **Runtime config vs file**: `openclaw gateway config get providers.bailian-embedding` — compare what the gateway sees vs what's in your file. The baseUrl might look correct in the file but fail at runtime (DNS, TLS, or cached config).

2. **Test endpoint directly**:
```
curl -X POST https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1/embeddings \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-YOUR_KEY" \
  -d '{"input": "test", "model": "text-embedding-v3"}'
```
If curl works but gateway doesn't, it's a config resolution issue.

3. **Check env var shadowing**: Gateway picks up env-sourced provider config on startup and can silently override file config. Any OPENCLAW_* env vars?

4. **Gateway logs**: `openclaw gateway logs --grep bailian` — look for the actual URL being hit at runtime.

The `disabled: true, unavailable: true` pattern means gateway fails the fetch on init and marks the provider dead. If it works via curl, the gateway is resolving a different endpoint than what you configured.

**Summary**
Keep open. Current main and the latest release still force the OpenAI provider lookup key in the memory embedding path, and the open linked PR is the right active fix path for this issue.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection on current main shows a custom memorySearch.provider can select the OpenAI adapter through models.providers.<id>.api, but the adapter and resolver then force the provider lookup key back to openai.

**Next step**
Do not queue a new repair lane because the open linked PR already owns the narrow fix path and closes this issue.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Land https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/81170 or an equivalent focused fix that preserves the requested provider ID at both OpenAI embedding boundaries, then close this issue after it reaches main.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes. Source inspection on current main shows a custom memorySearch.provider can select the OpenAI adapter through models.providers.<id>.api, but the adapter and resolver then force the provider lookup key back to openai.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes. Preserving options.provider ?? "openai" at both OpenAI embedding boundaries is the narrow maintainable fix because adapter identity stays openai while endpoint, auth, and cache lookup use the caller's configured provider ID.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: This is a confirmed provider-routing bug with a narrow memory-search surface and an open focused fix PR.
- `impact:auth-provider`: The bug makes memory embeddings resolve endpoint and API key data for openai instead of the configured custom provider.

What I checked:

- **adapter-drops-custom-provider-id:** Current main spreads caller options but then overwrites the provider with "openai" and records the cache provider as "openai", so a custom memorySearch.provider is lost before remote embedding setup. ([`extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.ts:24`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.ts#L24), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))
- **resolver-drops-custom-provider-id:** Current main passes provider: "openai" into resolveRemoteEmbeddingClient, so the downstream client cannot read the reporter's custom provider row. ([`extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts:98`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts#L98), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))
- **custom-provider-adapter-selection-supported:** The runtime already resolves lookup IDs from the requested provider plus its configured api owner, so a custom provider using api: "openai-completions" can select the OpenAI adapter if the original provider ID is preserved downstream. ([`src/plugins/memory-embedding-provider-runtime.ts:53`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/src/plugins/memory-embedding-provider-runtime.ts#L53), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))
- **remote-client-contract-needs-original-id:** The remote embedding client reads models.providers[params.provider] and resolves API keys for params.provider, so the provider lookup key must remain the custom ID to use its baseUrl, headers, and credentials. ([`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings-remote-client.ts:41`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings-remote-client.ts#L41), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))
- **docs-contract:** The memory configuration docs state that memorySearch.provider may be a configured models.providers.<id> entry and that OpenClaw preserves that custom provider ID for endpoint, auth, and model-prefix handling. Public docs: [`docs/reference/memory-config.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/reference/memory-config). ([`docs/reference/memory-config.md:91`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/reference/memory-config), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))
- **latest-release-still-affected:** The v2026.5.18 release has the same hard-coded provider: "openai" in both affected OpenAI embedding files, so the issue is not shipped as fixed. ([`extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.ts:24`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/50a2481652b6/extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.ts#L24), [50a2481652b6](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/50a2481652b6))

This PR adds an "Active Time" summary card to the usage overview, displaying the total active duration across sessions (`durationSumMs`) as the primary value and average session duration as the sub-label. It also moves the avg-session-duration sub-text out of the Error Rate card (replacing it with total message count, which is more contextually relevant) and adds a defensive `typeof process !== "undefined"` guard in `local-storage.ts` to prevent ReferenceErrors in environments without a global `process`. - New "Active Time" card surfaces `stats.durationSumMs` — a field already computed in `usage-metrics.ts` — without any data model changes. - The Error Rate card's sub-label is improved: `errors · avg session` → `errors · msgs`, which is more meaningful context for an error-rate metric. - The `local-storage.ts` fix is a correct defensive change guarding against environments where `process` is undefined (e.g., browser environments that don't shim `process`).

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it is a clean, additive UI change with a complementary defensive fix. - Both changes are small and well-scoped. The new "Active Time" card reuses an already-computed field (`durationSumMs`) and follows the exact same patterns as all other summary cards. The `local-storage.ts` fix only adds safety guards and does not change behaviour in any already-working environment. No new data sources, no API changes, no risk of regressions. - No files require special attention. Configure `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` with `tailscale.mode: "off"` (or unset mode) and start gateway. 2. Greptile Summary

This PR adds `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` / `--tailscale-control-url` to support self-hosted Tailscale-compatible control servers (e.g., Headscale). The implementation is correct and well-structured: validation is enforced at three layers (Zod schema, config-object validator, and runtime resolver), the merge logic mirrors the existing `mode`/`resetOnExit` pattern, tests cover all meaningful combinations, and the docs provide clear setup instructions with explicit limitations. **Key changes:** - `zod-schema.ts`: `controlUrl: z.string().url().optional()` added to the Tailscale config object. - `validation.ts`: `validateGatewayTailscaleControlUrl` rejects `serve`/`funnel` + `controlUrl` combinations at config-parse time. - `server-runtime-config.ts`: Runtime guard catches the same invalid combination for CLI-override paths that bypass file validation. - `startup-auth.ts`: `mergeGatewayTailscaleConfig` extended to propagate `controlUrl` from override to merged config. - `run.ts`: `--tailscale-control-url` flag with early URL-format validation via `new URL()`. - Tests: focused coverage in both the config-validation and runtime-resolver test suites. **One minor concern:** - Both `z.string().url()` (Zod) and `new URL()` (CLI) accept any URL scheme, including `ftp://`, `custom://`, etc. Since Headscale only speaks HTTP/HTTPS, consider restricting `controlUrl` to `http`/`https` schemes to surface misconfiguration earlier with a clear error.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; the new feature is purely additive, backward-compatible, and well-gated behind validation. - The implementation is clean and layered validation provides solid defence-in-depth. The only gap is that the URL scheme is not restricted to `http`/`https`, which could lead to confusing failures if a user passes a non-HTTP URL for `controlUrl`. All other logic is correct and fully tested. - `src/config/zod-schema.ts` and `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts` for the URL-scheme restriction noted above.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/config/zod-schema.ts Line: 703 Comment: **Scheme not restricted for `controlUrl`** `z.string().url()` (and `new URL()` in the CLI) accept any URL scheme, including `ftp://`, `custom://`, etc. Tailscale and Headscale control servers only support `http`/`https`, so a non-HTTP(S) URL would be silently accepted here but would fail at the Tailscale client level. Consider refining the Zod validation to enforce the scheme: ```suggestion controlUrl: z.string().url().refine((u) => /^https?:\/\//i.test(u), { message: "controlUrl must be an http or https URL", }).optional(), ``` The same guard should be applied in the CLI where `new URL(tailscaleControlUrlRaw)` is called — verify the parsed protocol is `"http:"` or `"https:"` after the `new URL()` succeeds. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

No change to default security model or existing single-host agents. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #12405 ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New `tools.exec.allowedHosts` config field — optional array of hosts an agent may request at runtime. - `exec-approvals.json` allowlist supports per-host map format: `{ "gateway": [...], "sandbox": [{ "pattern": "*" }] }`. - Legacy array format backward compatible, one-time warning at startup, `openclaw doctor --fix` auto-migrates to `{ "default": [...] }`. - Invalid `allowedHosts` combinations rejected at config-load with clear error messages. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) Yes - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: `allowedHosts` only allows going more isolated, never less. Validated at both config-load (Zod `superRefine`) and runtime (defense in depth): - `host: "sandbox"` cannot include `"gateway"` or `"node"` (container escape prevention) - `host: "gateway"` cannot include `"node"` (unknown trust boundary) - `host: "node"` can only include `"node"` Sandbox exec only enters allowlist processing when explicitly requested via `allowedHosts`. Default sandbox-without-sandbox-runtime (agents with no exec config) is unaffected. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Debian 12 - Runtime/container: Node 22, OpenClaw 2026.3.13 gateway, Docker sandbox - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel: Telegram - Relevant config: agent with `host: "gateway"`, `allowedHosts: ["gateway", "sandbox"]`, per-host allowlist map (gateway restricted, sandbox wildcard `*`) ### Steps 1. Configure agent with `allowedHosts: ["gateway", "sandbox"]` 2. Set per-host allowlist: gateway = `[gh, xurl]`, sandbox = `[{ "pattern": "*" }]` 3. Agent calls `exec(command="gh auth status", host="gateway")` 4. Agent calls `exec(command="echo hello", host="sandbox")` 5. Agent calls `exec(command="python3 --version", host="gateway")` 6. Set invalid config `host: "sandbox"`, `allowedHosts: ["sandbox", "gateway"]`, restart gateway ### Expected Steps 3-4 succeed, step 5 blocked by allowlist, step 6 rejected at config-load. ### Actual All cases pass as expected. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: gateway allowlist (gh ✅, python3 ❌), sandbox wildcard (echo ✅, curl ✅, Paperclip API ✅), main agent not broken ✅, Zod validation rejects all 3 invalid combos ✅, `openclaw doctor` detects legacy format ✅, `openclaw doctor --fix` migrates array → `{ "default": [...] }` ✅ - Edge cases checked: agent with no exec config (default sandbox fallback) still works, legacy array format backward compatible, wildcard `*` in sandbox allowlist, `allow-always` node-host call sites updated - What you did not verify: node host end-to-end (no node configured in test env), `allow-always` approval flow on gateway/sandbox (agent configured with `ask: "off"`) ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) No (legacy format works, warning + doctor migration available) ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `allowedHosts` from config — behavior reverts to single-host mode - Files/config to restore: `exec-approvals.json` — switch map back to array if needed - Known bad symptoms: `exec host not allowed` errors when agent requests a host not in `allowedHosts`, `allowlist miss` if per-host resolution returns wrong bucket ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: per-host allowlist map format could confuse existing tooling reading `exec-approvals.json` - Mitigation: legacy array format fully supported, auto-detection via `isAllowlistByHost()`, doctor migration available, one-time startup warning ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-16T18:47:10Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces `tools.exec.allowedHosts` — a per-host allowlist feature allowing agents to request a more-isolated exec host (e.g. sandbox) while remaining configured with a more-permissive default (e.g. gateway). The new per-host map format for `exec-approvals.json`, Zod validation, `openclaw doctor --fix` migration, and docs are all well-structured. **Key concerns:** - **Data corruption via unupdated `node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` call sites** — `addAllowlistEntry` (line 500) and `recordAllowlistUse` (lines 513–519) were not updated to pass the new `host` parameter. After `openclaw doctor --fix` migrates a node-host agent's flat array to `{ "default": [...] }`, a subsequent "allow-always" approval on the node host triggers the `addAllowlistEntry` path without `host`. Because `isAllowlistByHost` returns `true` but `host` is `undefined`, the function falls into the `else` branch where `Array.isArray(perHostMap)` is `false`, initializes `allowlist = []`, and saves `[newEntry]` — silently **overwriting the entire per-host map**. This is the most critical bug in the PR. - **`mergeLegacyAgent` silently drops per-host map data** — both `currentEntries` and `legacyEntries` are coerced to `[]` when the allowlist is an object (not an array). If one side of the merge was already doctor-migrated to map format, that data is lost. - **`resolveAllowlistForHost` empty-array semantics** — `??` only falls back on `null | undefined`, not on `[]`. An explicit `"gateway": []` bucket shadows the `"default"` fallback, contrary to the documented "falls back to default" behavior. This is worth a note in the docs or a code-level fix.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge — a pre-existing call site in `invoke-system-run.ts` was not updated and can corrupt per-host allowlist maps for any agent after a doctor migration. - The feature design and the gateway/sandbox paths are well-implemented, but the `addAllowlistEntry` call in `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` omits the new `host` parameter. For node-host agents migrated from legacy flat-array format (the intended migration path via `openclaw doctor --fix`), this call will silently overwrite the entire per-host map with a single-entry flat array on the first "allow-always" approval. This is a concrete, reproducible data-loss bug on the node-host path. The secondary concerns (mergeLegacyAgent, empty-array fallback semantics) are lower severity but add to the overall risk. - `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` (unupdated callers of `addAllowlistEntry` and `recordAllowlistUse`) and `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` (`mergeLegacyAgent` data loss and `resolveAllowlistForHost` empty-array semantics).

Comments Outside Diff (4)

 1. `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`, line 500 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts#L500)) **`addAllowlistEntry` missing `host` corrupts per-host allowlist map** This call omits the new `host` parameter introduced in this PR. When an agent uses the per-host map format (including any agent whose legacy flat array was migrated by `openclaw doctor --fix` to `{ "default": [...] }`), `isAllowlistByHost(existing.allowlist)` will return `true` but `host` will be `undefined`. Execution falls into the `else` branch of `addAllowlistEntry`, where `Array.isArray(existing.allowlist)` is `false`, so `allowlist` is forced to `[]`. The agent's entire per-host map is then **overwritten** with a flat single-entry array on the next save: ```ts // existing.allowlist = { "default": [{ pattern: "curl" }] } // After addAllowlistEntry(file, agentId, "gh") without host: // existing.allowlist = [{ id: "...", pattern: "gh" }] ← map is gone ``` This is triggered whenever a node-host agent with per-host allowlist format grants an "allow-always" approval. The fix is to pass `"node"` as the host: 2. `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`, line 513-519 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts#L513-L519)) **`recordAllowlistUse` missing `host` parameter** `recordAllowlistUse` was updated in this PR to accept an optional `host` parameter so it can update the correct per-host bucket. This call site in `invoke-system-run.ts` was not updated, so for agents with a per-host map allowlist (e.g. post-migration `{ "default": [...] }`), the use record will always be written to the `"default"` bucket regardless of which host actually ran the command. In the common migrated case this is harmless, but it's inconsistent with the gateway path which now passes `effectiveHost`. 3. `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`, line 831-839 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L831-L839)) **Empty-array host bucket silently shadows `"default"` fallback** `resolveAllowlistForHost` uses the nullish-coalescing operator (`??`), which only falls back when the left-hand operand is `null` or `undefined`. An explicitly empty array (`[]`) is neither, so a configuration like `{ "gateway": [], "default": [{ "pattern": "curl" }] }` would resolve to an **empty** allowlist for `"gateway"` (blocking all gateway commands) rather than falling back to `"default"`. The docs describe the resolution order as `allowlist[host]` → `allowlist["default"]` → empty, and the sentence "when no host-specific entry exists" could reasonably be interpreted as "when the array is empty OR the key is absent." Users who write `"gateway": []` intending "no special overrides, use the default" will be silently blocked instead. Consider documenting this edge case explicitly, or treating an empty array identically to an absent key: ```ts export function resolveAllowlistForHost( resolved: ExecApprovalsResolved, host: ExecHost, ): ExecAllowlistEntry[] { if (resolved.allowlistByHost) { const hostEntries = resolved.allowlistByHost[host]; // Treat an empty or absent host bucket as "fall back to default" if (hostEntries && hostEntries.length > 0) return hostEntries; return resolved.allowlistByHost["default"] ?? []; } return resolved.allowlist; } ``` 4. `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`, line 209-239 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L209-L239)) **`mergeLegacyAgent` silently drops per-host allowlist when merging** Both `currentEntries` and `legacyEntries` are set to `[]` when the respective allowlist is a per-host map (because `Array.isArray(obj)` is `false` for objects). Any per-host map data is therefore silently discarded if `mergeLegacyAgent` is ever called for an agent that has already been migrated to the map format. `mergeLegacyAgent` is called on line 331 when a `"default"` key in `agents` is being merged into the main agent entry during `normalizeExecApprovals`. If the primary agent entry uses a per-host map but the legacy `agents.default` still uses the flat array (or vice versa), the map data will be lost. While the current code path may make this rare in practice, the silent data loss is risky given that `openclaw doctor --fix` can upgrade agents to map format at any time. At minimum, consider logging a warning if either `current` or `legacy` has a per-host map allowlist when this merge function is invoked.

Execution falls into the `else` branch of `addAllowlistEntry`, where `Array.isArray(existing.allowlist)` is `false`, so `allowlist` is forced to `[]`. The agent's entire per-host map is then **overwritten** with a flat single-entry array on the next save: ```ts // existing.allowlist = { "default": [{ pattern: "curl" }] } // After addAllowlistEntry(file, agentId, "gh") without host: // existing.allowlist = [{ id: "...", pattern: "gh" }] ← map is gone ``` This is triggered whenever a node-host agent with per-host allowlist format grants an "allow-always" approval. The fix is to pass `"node"` as the host: ```suggestion addAllowlistEntry(phase.approvals.file, phase.agentId, pattern, "node"); ``` How can I resolve this? Greptile Summary

This PR adds `docs/concepts/multi-agent-patterns.md`, a new guide covering six production-oriented multi-agent orchestration patterns: hub-and-spoke, cost-tiered model routing, channel-bound specialists, proactive heartbeat monitoring, pipeline processing, and an anti-patterns reference. The guide is well-written, technically consistent with the existing `docs/concepts/multi-agent.md` reference, and all internal cross-links resolve to real pages. Key observations: - The `"24:00"` value used for `activeHours.end` in Pattern 4 is valid and explicitly supported per `docs/gateway/heartbeat.md`. - **Pattern 1 contains a config/diagram inconsistency**: the ASCII diagram shows the coordinator routing to `analyst`, `writer`, and `engineer`, but `engineer` is absent from the `tools.agentToAgent.allow` list. Readers who copy the config will find the coordinator unable to route to the engineer via agent-to-agent messaging. - All related doc links (`/concepts/multi-agent`, `/automation/cron-vs-heartbeat`, `/concepts/memory`, `/concepts/agent-workspace`) resolve to existing files.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge after fixing the Pattern 1 config inconsistency where `engineer` is omitted from the `agentToAgent` allow list despite the diagram showing coordinator→engineer routing. - The guide is mostly accurate and well-structured. One clear bug exists in the Pattern 1 example config: the coordinator is shown routing to the `engineer` specialist in the diagram, but `engineer` is not included in `tools.agentToAgent.allow`. This means the example config is broken as-written and will mislead users who copy it verbatim. The fix is trivial (add `"engineer"` to the allow list), but the inconsistency warrants attention before merging docs that will be read by many users setting up production systems. - docs/concepts/multi-agent-patterns.md — specifically the `tools.agentToAgent.allow` list in Pattern 1 (lines 60–62).
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: docs/concepts/multi-agent-patterns.md Line: 60-62 Comment: **`engineer` agent missing from `agentToAgent` allow list** The ASCII diagram at the top of this pattern explicitly shows the `coordinator` routing tasks to all three specialists — `analyst`, `writer`, and `engineer`. However, the `allow` list in `tools.agentToAgent` only includes `["coordinator", "analyst", "writer"]`, omitting `engineer`. As shown by the existing example in `docs/concepts/multi-agent.md`, `agentToAgent.allow` is a symmetric allowlist — agents not in it cannot participate in agent-to-agent communication. Anyone copying this config verbatim will find that the coordinator cannot route tasks to the `engineer` via agent-to-agent messaging, directly contradicting the hub-and-spoke diagram. Either add `"engineer"` to the allow list, or update the diagram and prose to clarify that the engineer is reached through a different mechanism (e.g., direct user interaction). ```suggestion allow: ["coordinator", "analyst", "writer", "engineer"], ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 48834 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48834 - Title: feat(feishu): enable /focus and /unfocus commands + fix ACP block delivery - State: OPEN - Author: David553 - Labels: channel: feishu, size: L, extensions: acpx, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary The core Feishu session binding adapter and subagent hooks were landed in #46819. This PR adds the remaining pieces to make `/focus` fully functional on Feishu: - **`/focus` and `/unfocus` commands**: Allow Feishu as a valid channel, add `resolveFeishuFocusConversationId` for stable conversation binding (rejects group chats), update command registry - **ACP block delivery fix**: Add `promoteAcpBlocksToFinals` dispatcher capability so Feishu can opt-in to promoting ACP blocks to finals (needed because Feishu suppresses block streaming), while preserving Discord's native block handling - **Gateway lifecycle**: Initialize thread binding manager on `startAccount`, clean up on `stopAccount` - **Config**: Add `threadBindings` schema (enabled, idleHours, maxAgeHours, spawnAcpSessions) - **Plugin SDK**: Export thread-bindings helpers for Feishu - **Provider prefix normalization**: Strip `feishu:`/`lark:` prefixes before resolving targets ## Changed files (16 files, +263/-18) | Area | Files | |------|-------| | /focus commands | `action-focus.ts`, `action-unfocus.ts`, `shared.ts`, `feishu-context.ts` (new), `channel-context.ts`, `session.ts`, `commands-registry.data.ts` | | ACP delivery | `dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`, `reply-dispatcher.ts`, `feishu/reply-dispatcher.ts` | | Gateway/config | `feishu/channel.ts`, `feishu/config-schema.ts` | | SDK/misc | `plugin-sdk/feishu.ts`, `session-binding-service.ts`, `acpx/process.ts` | ## Context Supersedes #40936 (clean rebase, same changes). ## Test plan - [x] `startup-memory` passes - [x] Local typecheck + lint + format pass - [x] `extension-fast (acpx)` passes - [x] Manual test: `/focus` in Feishu DM binds ACP session - [x] Manual test: ACP block replies reach Feishu user 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T07:45:00Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR completes Feishu's `/focus` and `/unfocus` integration by adding conversation-ID resolution, channel-surface detection, thread-binding lifecycle management in the gateway, and an ACP block-promotion mechanism (`promoteAcpBlocksToFinals`) to compensate for Feishu's suppression of block streaming. It also migrates the session-binding-adapter registry to `globalThis` to survive module-loading boundary splits, and strips the `CLAUDECODE` env var from spawned ACP child processes. - The new `resolveFeishuConversationId` logic correctly rejects bare group chats, strips known provider/type prefixes, and composes `chatId:topic:threadId` for topic threads. - The `promoteAcpBlocksToFinals` dispatcher flag is a clean opt-in; Discord's native block handling is unchanged. - `state.blockCount` is incremented even when a block payload is promoted to a final reply, which may mislead callers of `getBlockCount()` on Feishu sessions. - The `target` argument added to `/unfocus` is defensively useful but its `"Ignored"` description may surface confusingly in slash-command UIs. - The `resolveFeishuConversationId` function silently returns `undefined` for unknown `ChatType` / unrecognised ID prefixes with no diagnostic logging, which could complicate future debugging.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge; no critical runtime bugs were found, and the edge-case concerns are low-risk. - The implementation follows existing patterns (Telegram parity), `createFeishuThreadBindingManager` is idempotent on restart, and the `globalThis` adapter registry fix is correct. The `blockCount` vs. promoted-final discrepancy is a minor observability gap rather than a delivery bug, and the silent rejection in `resolveFeishuConversationId` for unknown types errs on the safe side. - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` — verify that no caller uses `getBlockCount()` to gate block-specific post-processing for Feishu sessions.

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 48877 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48877 - Title: feat(telegram): add multi-level menu support to customCommands - State: OPEN - Author: liangzr - Labels: channel: telegram, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Extends Telegram `customCommands` with optional `menus` and `routes` fields, enabling declarative multi-level inline keyboard menus without AI roundtrips. ## Motivation Currently, custom Telegram commands registered via `customCommands` only appear in the BotFather menu — the actual message is forwarded to the AI agent. This works for simple commands but is slow and wasteful for pure-navigation menus (multi-level menu trees with back buttons, sub-menus, etc.) that don't need AI processing. OpenClaw's built-in `/model` command already implements this pattern (multi-level inline keyboard with callback routing) but the capability isn't exposed to user-defined commands. ## Changes **4 files, +72 lines:** - `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` — Schema: adds optional `menus` and `routes` to `TelegramCustomCommandSchema` - `src/config/types.telegram.ts` — Type: updates `TelegramCustomCommand` with menu/route fields - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts` — Registers `bot.command()` handler for customCommands with menus, sending `menus.main` as inline keyboard - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.ts` — In `callback_query` handler, routes callback data matching `routes` config to menu navigation (editMessage), unmatched callbacks fall through to AI ## Config Example ```json { "customCommands": [{ "command": "work", "description": "Work menu", "menus": { "main": { "text": "⚡ *Work Menu*", "buttons": [ [{"text": "📊 Status", "callback_data": "w:status"}], [{"text": "🔧 Tools", "callback_data": "w:tools"}] ] }, "tools": { "text": "⚡ *Work Menu* › *Tools*", "buttons": [ [{"text": "◀️ Back", "callback_data": "w:nav:main"}] ] } }, "routes": { "w:tools": "tools", "w:nav:main": "main" } }] } ``` - `menus`: defines menu pages (`main` is the entry point) - `routes`: maps callback_data → menu name for navigation (handled at gateway level, no AI) - Callback data **not** in routes falls through to AI for action execution ## Behavior 1. User sends `/work` → bot responds instantly with `menus.main` inline keyboard 2. User clicks "Tools" (`w:tools`) → callback matches `routes`, message is edited to show `tools` menu 3. User clicks "Status" (`w:status`) → not in routes, forwarded to AI agent as a synthetic message 4. User clicks "Back" (`w:nav:main`) → callback matches routes, message edited back to main menu ## Testing Tested with a standalone OpenClaw instance + dedicated Telegram bot. Verified: - [x] `/test_menu` shows inline keyboard instantly - [x] Sub-menu navigation works (editMessage) - [x] Back button returns to main menu - [x] TypeScript compiles cleanly (`tsc --noEmit`) - [x] No changes to existing customCommand behavior (commands without menus work as before) ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T09:06:25Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR extends `customCommands` in the Telegram provider with optional `menus` and `routes` fields, enabling declarative multi-level inline keyboard menus that are handled at the gateway level without an AI roundtrip. The approach follows the pattern already used by the built-in `/model` command and is a clean, additive change with no impact on existing commands. Key findings from the review: - **Weak button schema validation** (`zod-schema.providers-core.ts`): The `buttons` field uses `z.array(z.array(z.any()))`, skipping per-button validation. Malformed button configs (missing `callback_data`, wrong types) pass Zod and only fail at runtime when Telegram rejects the API call. A structured `z.object({ text: z.string(), callback_data: z.string() })` schema would catch misconfiguration early. - **Silent callback data collision** (`bot-handlers.ts`): The routing loop breaks on the first command whose `routes` map contains the matching key. If two `customCommands` share the same callback_data string (e.g. both use `"back": "main"`), the second command's menu is silently never reached. There is no validation at config load time to detect this. The namespaced key convention in the PR description is the right instinct but isn't enforced. - **`(c: any)` casts** (`bot-handlers.ts`, `bot-native-commands.ts`): `TelegramCustomCommand` already carries the new optional fields after this PR, so the `any` casts are unnecessary and suppress type safety. - **Legacy `"Markdown"` parse mode**: Both new `sendMessage` and `editCallbackMessage` calls use the legacy `"Markdown"` mode. If dynamic content contains unescaped special characters, Telegram silently drops the message. `"MarkdownV2"` with proper escaping is the current recommendation.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge for basic use cases, but has a silent routing collision risk and weak schema validation that could cause hard-to-debug issues in multi-command configs. - The core feature logic is sound and the existing command behavior is unaffected. However, the lack of schema-level validation for button objects and the absence of uniqueness enforcement for route keys across commands are gaps that could produce silent runtime failures. These aren't blocking for a single-command setup (as tested), but become real issues when multiple menu commands are configured. - `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` (button schema strictness) and `extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.ts` (callback data collision logic).

Since the type in `types.telegram.ts` already defines the expected shape, the schema should reflect it: ```suggestion buttons: z.array( z.array( z.object({ text: z.string(), callback_data: z.string(), }), ), ), ``` How can I resolve this? GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 49063 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/49063 - Title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats - State: OPEN - Author: sonim1 - Labels: channel: telegram, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized correctly in channel relay setups. - Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly allowed. - What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the receiving account. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`, because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Integrations - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #49062 ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats. - Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."` - Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13 Operating system: all OS Install method: npm global Model: gpt-5.4 Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth ### Steps 1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed. 2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel. 3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior. ### Expected - Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status. ### Actual - Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post. - After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds. - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks. - Edge cases checked: - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands. - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists. - What you did not verify: - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes) - Config/env changes? (No) - Migration needed? (No) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false - Files/config to restore: - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command." - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None. - Risk: - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity. - Mitigation: - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender. - Explicit chat allow is still required. - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior. - Risk: - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post. - Mitigation: - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR. - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T14:32:30Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR enables native Telegram slash commands (e.g. `/status`) to be executed from `channel_post` updates by routing channel post authorization through the explicit chat allowlist instead of the unavailable human-sender identity. The change is well-scoped and correctly handles both `useAccessGroups = true` (requires explicit `groupConfig` entry) and `useAccessGroups = false` (open policy, still respects `commands.allowFrom`). Key changes: - `isChannelPostContext` helper detects channel post vs regular message contexts using grammY's `hasChatType` API with a `channelPost != null` fallback for tests. - `isChannelPost` flag now bypasses sender-based allowlist enforcement (`enforceAllowlistAuthorization`) and routes to a dedicated `commandAuthorizers` branch that uses `channelPolicy?.groupConfig` (explicit channel entry) as the sole auth source when `useAccessGroups = true`, and an empty authorizer array (open policy) when `useAccessGroups = false`. - The previously flagged `configured: true` hardcoding bug — which would have blocked channel posts under `useAccessGroups = false` — is fixed via the `useAccessGroups ? [...] : []` ternary. - `ctx.msg` is added as the top-priority message source in `buildTelegramUpdateKey` and `getTelegramSequentialKey`, aligning with grammY's canonical `ctx.msg` getter. - The test harness injects `msg` from `channelPost` to simulate grammY's `ctx.msg` in unit tests. - One new test covers the `commands.allowFrom.telegram = []` + `useAccessGroups = false` rejection path, but a positive test confirming that an explicitly configured channel can execute commands with `useAccessGroups = true` is missing.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge with minor gaps in test coverage for the positive channel-post command execution path. - The core authorization logic is correct and the previously flagged `configured: true` / `useAccessGroups = false` asymmetry has been addressed. Security is bounded by explicit chat allow checks. The only remaining concerns are: (1) `isGroup` semantic overloading which adds cognitive load for future contributors, and (2) the absence of a positive test for `useAccessGroups = true` with an explicitly configured channel — meaning a regression in `channelPolicy?.groupConfig` evaluation could go undetected. Neither is a blocking issue for the stated goal of this PR. - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts` lines 188 and 331–341 warrant a second look for the `isGroup` overloading and the `resolveGroupPolicy` double-call (the latter was already flagged in a previous review thread). `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.group-auth.test.ts` is missing the positive allow test for the `useAccessGroups=true` + explicit `groupConfig` path.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts Line: 188 Comment: **`isGroup` semantic overloading for channel posts** Setting `isGroup = isChannelPost || ...` reuses the group code path for channels, but `isGroup` is subsequently used in several places where the "group" semantics do not cleanly apply to channels (e.g. `storeAllowFrom`, `effectiveDmPolicy`, `ChatType`, `senderAllowed`/`groupSenderAllowed` computations). All of those are either harmless dead code for channel posts or intentionally reused, but it makes the variable's meaning opaque for future contributors. Since `isChannelPost` is already tracked separately and the channel-specific `commandAuthorizers` branch short-circuits before any of the group-sender paths matter, consider keeping `isGroup` restricted to actual group/supergroup types and passing `isChannelPost` explicitly where group-like routing is needed: ```typescript const isGroup = msg.chat.type === "group" || msg.chat.type === "supergroup"; // Channel posts share group-like routing for thread resolution, policy lookup, etc. const isGroupOrChannel = isGroup || isChannelPost; ``` This makes it unambiguous which downstream usages are intentional. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.group-auth.test.ts Line: 197-230 Comment: **Missing positive test for explicitly-allowed channel with `useAccessGroups=true`** The only channel-post test in this file verifies the rejection path (`commands.allowFrom.telegram = []`, `useAccessGroups = false`). There is no test that confirms the happy path: an explicitly configured channel (i.e. `resolveGroupPolicy` returns a `groupConfig`) can actually execute native commands when `useAccessGroups = true`. Without this test, the entire positive flow — `channelPolicy?.groupConfig` being truthy, `commandAuthorizers = [{ configured: true, allowed: true }]`, `resolveCommandAuthorizedFromAuthorizers` returning `true` — goes unverified. A regression in `resolveGroupPolicy`'s `groupConfig` shape or in the `Boolean(channelPolicy?.groupConfig)` evaluation would be silently missed. Consider adding a paired test along these lines: ```typescript it("allows native commands from an explicitly configured channel when useAccessGroups is true", async () => { const { handlers, sendMessage } = setup({ useAccessGroups: true, resolveGroupPolicy: () => ({ allowlistEnabled: true, allowed: true, groupConfig: { agentId: "default" }, }) as ChannelGroupPolicy, }); await handlers.status?.({ channelPost: { chat: { id: TEST_CHANNEL_ID, type: "channel", title: "Bot Relay" }, sender_chat: { id: TEST_CHANNEL_ID, type: "channel", title: "Bot Relay" }, message_id: 1, date: 1700000000, text: "/status", }, match: "", }); const notAuthCalls = findNotAuthorizedCalls(sendMessage); expect(notAuthCalls).toHaveLength(0); }); ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #49120: fix(security): reject private/internal hosts in cron webhook URLs
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 49120 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/49120 - Title: fix(security): reject private/internal hosts in cron webhook URLs - State: OPEN - Author: frankentini - Labels: size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem `normalizeHttpWebhookUrl` only validated that webhook URLs used http(s) protocol, but did not check whether the hostname pointed to private, loopback, link-local, or other special-use addresses. A cron job configured with `delivery.mode="webhook"` could target: - `localhost` / `127.0.0.1` / `[::1]` - RFC 1918 private ranges (`10.x`, `172.16.x`, `192.168.x`) - Cloud metadata endpoints (`169.254.169.254`, `metadata.google.internal`) - `.local` / `.internal` hostnames While the runtime fetch layer performs DNS-pinned SSRF validation, rejecting these at URL creation time prevents dangerous URLs from being persisted in cron job configs in the first place. ## Fix Import and apply the existing `isBlockedHostnameOrIp` guard from the SSRF module (`src/infra/net/ssrf.ts`) inside `normalizeHttpWebhookUrl`. This provides defense-in-depth: blocked URLs are rejected early during cron job creation/patching, before any network request is attempted. ## Testing - Added `src/cron/webhook-url.test.ts` with 15 test cases covering protocol validation, SSRF hostname/IP rejection, and public URL acceptance - Verified existing `service.jobs.test.ts` (35 tests) and `normalize.test.ts` (30 tests) continue to pass - [x] AI-assisted, fully tested - [x] Understand what the code does ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T16:16:45Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds defense-in-depth SSRF protection to `normalizeHttpWebhookUrl` by calling the existing `isBlockedHostnameOrIp` guard before a cron webhook URL is persisted, preventing private/loopback/link-local/special-use addresses from ever reaching the runtime fetch layer. - **Implementation** (`src/cron/webhook-url.ts`): One import and a four-line guard inserted at exactly the right point — after protocol validation, before returning the normalized URL. `parsed.hostname` (which the WHATWG `URL` constructor already de-brackets for IPv6 literals) is passed directly to `isBlockedHostnameOrIp` with no explicit policy, correctly defaulting to the strictest behaviour. The existing `try/catch` means malformed URLs still fall through to `null` cleanly. - **Tests** (`src/cron/webhook-url.test.ts`): 15 well-organised cases cover protocol rejection, SSRF categories (loopback, RFC 1918, link-local, cloud metadata, `.local`/`.internal`), and public IP acceptance. A few edge cases exercised by the SSRF module itself (e.g. `0.0.0.0`, IPv4-mapped-in-IPv6 like `::ffff:127.0.0.1`) are not mirrored here — not a bug, but worth adding as a standalone contract. - The PR correctly notes this is a *static* pre-persistence check and that the runtime DNS-pinned SSRF validation must remain in place to handle DNS-rebinding attacks.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- Safe to merge — the change is a focused, additive security improvement with no behavioural regressions. - The diff is small and well-scoped: it reuses an already-audited SSRF guard rather than reimplementing logic, inserts the check at the correct point in the validation flow, and is backed by a dedicated test file. The single style-level suggestion (extra edge-case tests) does not affect correctness. - No files require special attention; the only suggestion is optional additional test coverage in `src/cron/webhook-url.test.ts`.

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug where expired `.deleted.*` and `.reset.*` session archive artifacts could accumulate indefinitely in a session store running in `enforce` maintenance mode. The root cause was that archive cleanup only ran when the same save operation had also archived at least one live session — if no live sessions were pruned, stale archives were never touched. Additionally, older filename variants using `+HH-MM` timezone offsets or `YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` compact format were not recognized by the timestamp parser, making them ineligible for retention-based cleanup even when they should have been removed. Key changes: - **`store.ts`**: The archive cleanup gate is changed from `archivedDirs.size > 0 || resetArchiveRetentionMs != null` to `pruneAfterMs >= 0 || resetArchiveRetentionMs != null`. Since `pruneAfterMs` is always positive in practice, cleanup now runs unconditionally during every enforced-maintenance save. The `targetDirs` list always includes the store directory alongside any additional archived directories. - **`artifacts.ts`**: `ISO_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` is expanded to accept `±HH-MM`-style timezone suffixes, and a new `COMPACT_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` (`/^\d{8}-\d{6}$/`) handles `YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` legacy filenames. `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` now normalizes both formats to valid ISO 8601 strings before `Date.parse`. Note: the original split-on-`T` fallback branch at the bottom of `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` is now unreachable by any timestamp accepted by `isArchiveTimestamp` and can be treated as dead code. - **Tests**: A new integration test covers the exact bug scenario (cleanup fires even when no live sessions were pruned), and unit tests verify round-trip parsing for both new legacy formats.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; the fix correctly addresses the cleanup gap with no risk of over-deletion for recent archives. - The logic change is sound and well-tested. The new gate condition (`pruneAfterMs >= 0`) is effectively always true, which is intentional — the `cleanupArchivedSessionTranscripts` function's own guard (`olderThanMs < 0` early return) ensures no unsafe deletion occurs. The timestamp parsing additions are carefully scoped and validated by new unit tests. The main minor concern is that the always-true condition is slightly misleading, and the original fallback branch in `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` is now dead code — neither is a bug. - `src/config/sessions/store.ts` lines 482–483 (always-true gate condition worth clarifying) and `src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts` lines 63–67 (now-unreachable fallback branch).

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts`, line 63-67 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/9d14a02307762e4420cde3f99df7ac0c863c1f22/src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts#L63-L67)) **Fallback branch is now dead code** After adding the two new branches (`compact` and `iso`), the original fallback path (split on `"T"` and replace `-` with `:`) can no longer be reached by any timestamp that passes `isArchiveTimestamp()`. The `ISO_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` requires the zone suffix `(Z|[+-]\d{2}-\d{2})`, and both new branches exhaustively handle every format that regex accepts. A raw value like `2026-02-23T12-34-56` (no zone) wouldn't pass `isArchiveTimestamp`, so it would never arrive here. The code is harmless, but it gives a false impression that there's a third format to handle. If this is intentional as a final safety net, a brief comment would help; otherwise it's a candidate for removal to reduce surface area.
