Problem: Discord-native system messages such as channel rename/icon updates were not consistently recognized as system events, so some of them could fall through inbound preflight as ordinary user text. - Why it matters: this lets Discord-generated metadata reach the agent as if it were user intent, which is noisy and behaviorally incorrect. - What changed: this PR expands Discord system-message handling to cover the current native `MessageType` surface, routes those messages through queued system events, and adds regression coverage for both the mapping layer and the reported channel rename case. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change Discord routing/session logic, prompt formatting, or any non-Discord channel behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #24108 - Related #24108 ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Discord-native system messages such as channel rename/icon changes, calls, discovery warnings, invite reminders, role/app subscription notices, and similar Discord-generated events are now queued as `Discord system: ...` events during inbound preflight instead of being treated like ordinary inbound message text. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local repo checkout - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Discord - Relevant config (redacted): default Discord preflight test helpers / no special config required ### Steps 1. Receive a Discord-native system message such as `ChannelNameChange`. 2. Run inbound Discord preflight for that message. 3. Observe whether the message is dropped into `enqueueSystemEvent()` or allowed to continue as normal inbound text. ### Expected - Discord-native system messages are converted into `Discord system: ...` text and queued via preflight. - The reported channel rename case does not continue through the normal inbound message path. ### Actual - Before this change, `ChannelNameChange` was not handled in the Discord system-event mapper, so the message could fall through as ordinary inbound text. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Additional notes: - Added `extensions/discord/src/monitor/system-events.test.ts` for the audited mapping surface. - Added a rename regression in `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts`. - Direct local mapper verification confirmed `ChannelNameChange` now resolves to `Discord system: alice renamed the channel in Guild One #general`. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: direct local verification of the Discord system-event mapper for `ChannelNameChange`; static review of the preflight path confirming `resolveDiscordSystemEvent()` is the gate that enqueues and drops these messages. - Edge cases checked: rename handling, icon-change coverage, and the broader current Discord native system-message enum surface. - What you did **not** verify: I could not complete a clean local Vitest run in this environment because the local runner did not exit cleanly after startup, and `pnpm build` is currently blocked by an unrelated existing circular re-export in `src/plugin-sdk/synology-chat.ts`. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit. - Files/config to restore: `extensions/discord/src/monitor/system-events.ts` and the accompanying tests/changelog entry. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Discord-native system messages unexpectedly appearing again as ordinary inbound text, or over-broad classification of user-authored Discord message types. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: over-classifying a Discord message type that should remain user-authored content. - Mitigation: keep `Default`, `Reply`, `ThreadStarterMessage`, `ChatInputCommand`, and `ContextMenuCommand` as passthroughs and cover the audited system-only cases in tests. lobster-biscuit ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - yang-he at 2026-03-17T23:42:16Z: I investigated the current CI failures on this PR. The `install-smoke` log also shows the Docker build failing during `pnpm build:docker`, consistent with that baseline build breakage. - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T23:44:23Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug where Discord-native system messages (channel renames, icon changes, calls, invite reminders, discovery events, stage events, etc.) could fall through inbound preflight as ordinary user text instead of being routed to the system-event queue. It expands `resolveDiscordSystemEvent` to cover the current Discord `MessageType` surface and adds thorough regression coverage at both the unit and integration levels. Key changes: - `system-events.ts`: Adds ~15 new `MessageType` cases to the switch, all correctly delegating to the existing `buildDiscordSystemEvent` helper. The `default: return null` fallback preserves the conservative pass-through for unknown/future types. - `system-events.test.ts` (new): Covers all 33 audited system types and the 5 intended user-authored passthroughs. Minor testing quality concern: 33 assertions run in a single `for` loop inside one `it` block — using `it.each` would give one named test per case, making future regressions easier to pinpoint. - `message-handler.preflight.test.ts`: Adds a clean end-to-end regression for `ChannelNameChange`, verifying that `preflightDiscordMessage` returns `null` and `enqueueSystemEvent` is called with the correct human-readable text and `contextKey`. - `message-handler.preflight.test-helpers.ts`: Backward-compatible `type` field addition to `createDiscordMessage`, defaulting to `MessageType.Default`.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it tightens a security/noise boundary without touching routing, session logic, or non-Discord paths. - The logic is correct and well-contained. All new switch cases delegate to the pre-existing, tested `buildDiscordSystemEvent` helper, and the author-null guard is already in place. The only deduction is for a test quality concern (for-loop over 33 assertions in a single `it` block gives poor failure diagnostics) rather than any functional issue. The integration test correctly mocks `enqueueSystemEvent` via `vi.hoisted` and asserts the exact call signature, and the `default: return null` fallback conservatively handles any future unrecognised message types. - No files require special attention, though `system-events.test.ts` would benefit from switching to `it.each` for the 33-case loop to improve regression diagnostics.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: extensions/discord/src/monitor/system-events.test.ts Line: 59-63 Comment: **Poor failure diagnostics in loop-based test** When any one of the 33 assertions in this `for` loop fails, vitest reports the mismatched value but does not tell you *which* `MessageType` triggered the failure. You have to manually match the expected string back to a case, which becomes especially tedious if multiple cases ever regress simultaneously. Consider switching to `it.each` (vitest supports `describe.each` / `it.each` natively): ```ts it.each([ [MessageType.Call, "started a call"], [MessageType.ChannelNameChange, "renamed the channel"], // ... ] as const)("maps %s → '%s'", (type, action) => { expect(resolveDiscordSystemEvent(createMessage(type), location)).toBe( `Discord system: alice#1234 ${action} in ${location}`, ); }); ``` Each case then runs as a separate named test, making CI output and local debugging much clearer. - State: OPEN

- Labels: size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, status: 📣 needs proof

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Keep the TUI visibly busy while a run is waiting on a follow-up event after `sessions_yield`.

Before this change, the TUI could appear idle even though work was still effectively in flight. The gap showed up when the final assistant message contained the `sessions_yield` follow-up context marker: the run had ended intentionally, but the UI status dropped back to an idle-looking state.

## What changed

- add `awaiting follow-up` as a busy TUI activity state
- detect the `sessions_yield` follow-up marker in final assistant output
- preserve `awaiting follow-up` when reloading session history
- clear stale `awaiting follow-up` state back to `idle` when history no longer contains the marker
- add focused tests for both live event handling and history restore behavior

## Files

- `src/tui/tui.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts`

## Validation

- direct smoke harness passed for:
  - final event -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - history restore -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - stale follow-up state -> `idle`
- local lint/format checks passed on the touched files

## Notes

A focused local Vitest run hit a Node internal assertion in this environment, so the behavior was additionally checked with a direct harness against the patched TUI logic.
```

Today they must parse JSONL transcripts after the fact. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #51667: Feature: Native Audio Input for Omni-Modal Models (skip STT transcription)
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 51667
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51667
- Title: Feature: Native Audio Input for Omni-Modal Models (skip STT transcription)
- State: OPEN
- Author: jojo2a
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:security, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When using omni-modal models (e.g. `mimo-v2-omni`, Gemini 3 Flash) that natively support audio input, OpenClaw should be able to send voice notes directly to the model instead of routing them through Whisper/transcription first.

## Current Behavior

- User sends a voice note (WhatsApp/Telegram/etc.)
- OpenClaw downloads the audio file
- Audio is sent to a transcription provider (Whisper, Deepgram, OpenAI transcribe)
- Transcript text is injected into the agent context
- **Result:** Tone, emotion, prosody, and multi-speaker separation are lost

## Desired Behavior

Add a configuration option (e.g. `tools.media.audio.native: true`) that, when enabled:

1. Detects the audio MIME type
2. Sends the audio data directly to the configured omni-modal model as a native media part (same way images are handled)
3. The model processes the audio natively — preserving tone, emotion, and speaker diarization

## Why This Matters

- **Latency:** No intermediate STT step = faster response
- **Richness:** Preserves emotional context, tone, prosody that transcription discards
- **Multi-speaker:** Native omni-modal models can separate speakers; Whisper cannot
- **Model capabilities:** Models like mimo-v2-omni and Gemini 3 Flash are designed for native audio — this feature unlocks their full potential

## Use Case

Running mimo-v2-omni as the primary model via OpenRouter. The model supports native audio with speaker diarization, but OpenClaw currently forces all voice notes through Whisper, wasting the model omni-modal capabilities.

## Proposed Config

```json5
{
  tools: {
    media: {
      audio: {
        native: true, // Send audio directly to omni-modal model instead of transcribing
        fallbackToTranscription: true // Fall back to Whisper if model does not support audio
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Related

- Original issue: #1732 (closed as stale)

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.3.11
- Model: `openrouter/xiaomi/mimo-v2-omni`
- Channel: WhatsApp
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Ryce at 2026-03-21T15:19:56Z:
This is a great feature request — omni-modal models that process audio natively bypass the STT transcription step entirely, which creates a verification and continuity gap.

**The core problem with bypassing STT:**

When OpenClaw uses STT to transcribe audio, the transcript becomes part of the session transcript — it is a durable, searchable record of what was said. When the model processes audio directly, the session transcript shows only the model's *interpretation* of the audio, not the audio itself or any transcription.

This means:

1. **No verifiable record of what was heard** — if the model misinterprets an accent, mumbles, or background noise, there is no transcript to audit against
2. **Session continuity breaks on context loss** — if the session is reset or restored from snapshot, the "heard" audio is gone; only the model's summary remains
3. **Audit trail is one step removed from ground truth** — the session transcript says "user said X" but X is already the model's interpretation, not the original

**Where KeepMyClaw fits:**

KeepMyClaw snapshots capture the complete session context at defined intervals. When an omni-modal model processes audio natively, the snapshot should record:

- The audio blob itself (or a hash + reference) so the original is verifiable
- The model's transcription/interpretation so the delta between heard and interpreted is auditable
- The full audio context (surrounding messages, speaker identity) for replay

Without this, omni-modal audio processing trades transcript verifiability for speed — useful in the happy path, dangerous when the session needs to be audited or restored.

**A practical addition:**

Even without full audio archival, a "last-audio-transcript-delta" field in the snapshot would surface cases where the model's interpretation significantly diverged from what a human would expect — useful for catching hallucinated transcriptions of accented speech, technical terms, or noisy environments.

https://keepmyclaw.com

- jojo2a at 2026-03-23T12:55:35Z:
Thanks for the thoughtful analysis, Ryce. The audit trail concern is valid — but I think there's a pragmatic middle ground.

My primary use case: **voice messaging via WhatsApp/Telegram**. Users send voice notes expecting the assistant to *hear* them, not to read a butchered transcription. With Whisper (even large-v3), multi-speaker accents, background noise, and emotional tone are systematically lost. The model working with native audio produces dramatically better responses — even if the transcript is an interpretation.

**Pragmatic solution:**

1. Add a config flag `tools.media.audio.native: true` (opt-in, not default)
2. When enabled, send raw audio to omni-modal models (mimo-v2-omni, Gemini 3.x Flash, etc.) — skip STT entirely
3. Log a minimal metadata entry in the session: `{"audio": "<hash>", "duration": "12s", "model_interpretation": "..."}` — enough for audit without storing full blobs
4. Fallback to STT if the model doesn't support audio input

This is **not** about replacing STT for all cases. It's about giving users a choice: fast & rich (native audio) vs. verifiable & searchable (STT transcript).

**Why now:** Omni-modal models are becoming the default. Gemini 3.x Flash, MiMo-V2-Omni, and the upcoming GPT-5.4 all process audio natively. OpenClaw should support this natively instead of forcing every audio through Whisper as a bottleneck.

Happy to test with my setup (mimo-v2-omni on TrueNAS) and provide feedback.

- Hollychou924 at 2026-03-22T11:20:52Z:
## Analysis: ElevenLabs generates audio but OpenAI voice plays — provider selection race or fallback silent override

The key diagnostic clue from the reporter:

> `openclaw status` shows **Provider: elevenlabs (configured)** but **Last attempt shows Provider: openai`**

This tells us ElevenLabs is correctly configured but the **runtime delivery path silently switched to OpenAI**. Two likely causes:

### Cause A — Silent fallback on ElevenLabs failure (most likely)

The TTS pipeline calls ElevenLabs successfully (confirmed by portal showing API calls), but something in the OpenClaw audio delivery path fails — e.g., the audio format returned by ElevenLabs isn't handled correctly, the file write path fails, or the playback step gets a null result. The pipeline then silently falls back to OpenAI.

**Diagnostic:** Check if `messages.tts.fallback` is configured (or defaults to `openai`). If so, any post-generation error triggers the fallback without surfacing the original error.

**Fix:** The fallback should log a warning when it activates — e.g.:
```
[tts] ElevenLabs generation succeeded but delivery failed (reason: <error>) — falling back to openai
```
Currently this transition is silent.

### Cause B — Provider selection logic reads stale/wrong provider at delivery time

The TTS system may select the provider at two different points:
1. At generation time (correctly reads `elevenlabs` from config)
2. At playback/delivery time (reads a different value — possibly the session's last-used provider)

If the delivery path has a separate provider lookup that isn't synced with the generation path, it can generate ElevenLabs audio and then discard it, playing OpenAI audio instead.

### What to check

```bash
# Check if fallback is silently triggering
grep -i 'tts\|elevenlabs\|fallback' ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log | tail -50

# Check what audio file is actually being sent
# ElevenLabs returns MP3; OpenAI returns MP3 or OPUS
# The file format of what gets played would confirm which provider's output was used
```

### Fix

1. Make fallback activation visible: always log when fallback triggers and why
2. Audit TTS provider selection: ensure the same provider is used for both generation AND delivery, not read separately at each step
3. If ElevenLabs audio is generated successfully, it should always be used — fallback should only trigger if generation itself fails

- Artyomkun at 2026-03-22T22:39:37Z:
@Hollychou924 Great analysis — this is a silent fallback that's confusing users.

@steipete 

**Key diagnostic:**
- ElevenLabs API calls are logged on the provider side (so generation succeeded)
- But what gets played is OpenAI (so fallback triggered)

**Likely cause:**
Post‑generation step fails — maybe the audio format, file write, or playback path. Fallback kicks in without logging, so user only sees "ElevenLabs is configured, but I hear OpenAI."

**Fix suggestions:**
1. **Log fallback activation** — at least a warning with the reason
2. **Check format compatibility** — if ElevenLabs returns something OpenClaw can't play, convert or warn
3. **Single provider selection** — ensure the same provider is used for both generation and delivery, not read separately

**For debugging:**
- Compare the audio file that was actually sent (MP3 from ElevenLabs vs OpenAI)
- Check if `messages.tts.fallback` is set (or defaults to OpenAI)
- Look for any errors after "ElevenLabs generation succeeded" in logs

**Impact:**
This makes users think ElevenLabs is broken, even though it's working and billing. A clear warning would save them hours of config checking.

Thanks for the detailed analysis!

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 52244
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/52244
- Title: fix(openrouter): resolve thinking/reasoning defaults for dynamic models
- State: CLOSED
- Author: jyrkihuhta
- Labels: cli, agents, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, status: 📣 needs proof

Body:
```markdown
# fix(openrouter): resolve thinking/reasoning defaults for dynamic models

## Summary

- OpenRouter models not in the static catalog (e.g. `moonshotai/kimi-k2.5`) have both thinking and reasoning defaults fall to `"off"`, hiding reasoning output in the TUI even when the model returns `reasoning_content`
- Add `augmentModelCatalog` hook to surface cached OpenRouter model capabilities in the catalog, so `resolveReasoningDefault` can find dynamic models with `reasoning: true`
- Add `resolveDefaultThinkingLevel` hook with fallback to the capability cache for models not yet in the static catalog
- Export `getAllCachedOpenRouterModels` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-stream`

## Problem

When using an OpenRouter reasoning model that isn't in the static `models.json` catalog (any model resolved dynamically via `resolveDynamicModel`), the thinking/reasoning default resolution chain finds no catalog entry:

1. `resolveThinkingDefault` checks per-model config → global config → provider `resolveDefaultThinkingLevel` (not implemented) → catalog (model not found) → falls to `"off"`
2. `resolveReasoningDefault` checks catalog only (no provider hook) → model not found → falls to `"off"`

With both defaults at `"off"`, `reasoningMode` is `"off"`, so `streamReasoning=false` and `includeReasoning=false`. The pi-ai library correctly parses `reasoning_content` from the OpenRouter response into thinking blocks, but the subscribe handler silently drops them.

The user sees only the brief `content` field (or nothing if `content` is null), making it appear the model returned an empty response.

## Fix

The OpenRouter capability cache already has the `reasoning: boolean` flag for every model in the OpenRouter catalog (fetched at startup). This PR wires that data into the two default-resolution paths:

1. **`augmentModelCatalog`** — injects supplemental catalog rows from the capability cache, so `resolveReasoningDefault`'s catalog lookup succeeds for dynamic models
2. **`resolveDefaultThinkingLevel`** — returns `"low"` for reasoning-capable models, with fallback to the capability cache when the catalog entry doesn't have a `reasoning` hint yet

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/openrouter-model-capabilities.test.ts` passes (2 tests)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — no new errors (all errors are pre-existing on `main`)
- [x] `pnpm format` — clean
- [x] Manual verification: with kimi-k2.5 via OpenRouter, reasoning blocks now display in the TUI instead of being silently dropped

---

> [!NOTE]
> **AI-assisted:** Built with Claude Code (claude.ai/code). Code reviewed and tested by the author. Full understanding of the thinking/reasoning default resolution chain and how the capability cache integrates with the catalog.

Labels: cli, agents, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, status: 📣 needs proof
```

**src/agents/acp-spawn.ts** - Replaces the hard-coded `requesterIsSubagentSession` gate with `progressForwardEnabled` (`cfg.acp?.progressForward !== false`) in the `implicitStreamToParent` condition. - Keeps the existing heartbeat prerequisites in place: implicit forwarding still requires heartbeat to be enabled for the requester session and a usable session-local heartbeat relay route. - Hardens the thread-bound guards so implicit forwarding is blocked not only for explicit thread context, but also when the parent session is thread-bound via: - an active `targetKind === "session"` binding - a `:thread:` / `:topic:` marker in the session key - `deliveryContext.threadId` for suffixless threaded sessions (for example Discord threads that reuse the base session key) - **src/config/types.acp.ts**: Adds `progressForward?: boolean` to the ACP config type with JSDoc. - **src/config/zod-schema.ts**: Adds `progressForward: z.boolean().optional()` to the ACP config schema. - **src/config/schema.help.ts** / **src/config/schema.labels.ts**: Adds help text and label for `acp.progressForward`. - **docs.acp.md** - Documents the progress forwarding feature, what gets forwarded, how to disable it, and relay tuning options. - Clarifies that implicit forwarding also depends on heartbeat being enabled and a usable session-local heartbeat relay route. - **docs/.generated/config-baseline.json** / **docs/.generated/config-baseline.jsonl**: Regenerated to include the new config field. - **src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts** - Adds regression coverage for `acp.progressForward: false`. - Adds regression coverage for session-key thread markers. - Strengthens the thread-binding tests so they exercise the new guards instead of passing early on the heartbeat gate. - Adds regression coverage for suffixless threaded sessions where thread state is only visible through delivery context. ## Motivation Previously, progress forwarding was only implicitly enabled for subagent sessions with specific heartbeat conditions. This made it difficult to get visibility into long-running ACP runs without explicitly passing `streamTo="parent"` every time. By making it default-on (with a clean opt-out via `acp.progressForward: false`), users get transparent progress updates without additional configuration. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #53250: [UX] Exec approval timeout message should include Control UI link and setup hints
github_context:
GitHub item:

- Type: github_issue

- Title: [UX] Exec approval timeout message should include Control UI link and setup hints
- State: OPEN
- Author: yurtzy
- Labels: P3, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
When an exec command times out waiting for approval, the error message is generic:

```
Exec approval is required, but chat exec approvals are not enabled on Discord.
Approve it from the Web UI or terminal UI, or from Discord or Telegram if those approval clients are enabled.
```

This tells the user *that* approval is needed but gives no concrete next steps:
- No link to the Control UI
- No hint where the "terminal UI" is
- No way to know if Discord approvals are actually enabled
- No command to list pending approvals

As a result, operators get stuck in loops of retrying and timing out, especially during initial setup.

**Proposed improvement:**
Include specific, actionable guidance in the error message:
- The Control UI URL (if discoverable from gateway, e.g., `http://localhost:8080`) and how to get the token (`openclaw dashboard --no-open`)
- Reminder to check `openclaw.json` → `channels.discord.execApprovals.enabled`
- Suggest `openclaw exec-approvals list` if such CLI exists

Example:
```
Exec approval required.
→ Open Control UI: http://localhost:8080 (token from `openclaw dashboard --no-open`)
→ Or enable Discord approvals: set channels.discord.execApprovals.enabled=true and restart.
→ Or check pending: openclaw exec-approvals list
```

This small UX improvement reduces onboarding friction dramatically.

**I can submit a PR for this if desired.**
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #53406: feat: Let users steer running ACP/sub-agent sessions from Discord (and other chat surfaces)
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 53406
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/53406
- Title: feat: Let users steer running ACP/sub-agent sessions from Discord (and other chat surfaces)
- State: OPEN
- Author: ubehera
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
Right now, when I spawn a Codex or Claude Code session from Discord, I can see what the agent is doing (especially with `/reasoning on`), but I can't talk to it while it's working. If it goes off track, my only option is to kill it and start over.

In a terminal, Claude Code and Codex let you type corrections mid-task, answer questions the agent asks, and redirect it on the fly. The output streaming already works in Discord. The missing piece is the input side.

**Proposed behavior:**

When an ACP session is running in a Discord thread, messages sent in that thread should go to the running agent as input while it's working, not queue up for after it finishes.

Specifically:
- Send a message, the agent gets it while it's working (not after the current turn ends)
- Users can correct, redirect, or answer questions without killing the session
- Works for both ACP (Codex, Claude Code) and sub-agent sessions
- Output streaming already works today via `/reasoning on`. This just adds the input path

**Current workarounds:**

- Having the orchestrating agent relay messages back and forth using `process submit`, which is clunky and adds latency
- Using `openclaw tui` in a terminal, which works but defeats the point of having Discord integration
- Killing and re-spawning when something goes wrong, losing all progress

**Related issues:**

- #23580 (ACP thread-bound agents, merged)
- #28511 (ACP runtime plugin)
- #28484 (ACP file write bug in Discord threads)

The thread-bound session infrastructure and output streaming already exist. This is about completing the loop by adding interactive input.
```

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 55211 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/55211 - Title: fix: prevent re-entrant loop in internal hook trigger - State: OPEN - Author: ggzeng - Labels: size: S, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look Body: ```markdown ## Summary Fixes re-entrant infinite loop in `triggerInternalHook` when a handler calls `triggerInternalHook` again with the same `type:action:sessionKey` combination. ## Problem Before this fix, if a hook handler (e.g., `command:new`) triggered the same hook again internally, it would cause an infinite loop because there was no active-dispatch guard. ## Solution Added an `AsyncLocalStorage`-based re-entrancy guard: - Re-entrant calls within the same async call chain are blocked (handler → triggerInternalHook → same key → sees inherited store → dropped) - Independent concurrent calls from different async contexts are NOT blocked (preserves at-least-once delivery) ## Changes - `src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`: Added `AsyncLocalStorage` guard around `triggerInternalHook` - `src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts`: Added 116 lines of tests covering recursive, sequential, and concurrent dispatch behavior ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** Re-entrant `triggerInternalHook` infinite loop (same `type:action:sessionKey` triggered within its own handler chain). **Real environment tested:** Local dev checkout, Node v24.14.0 on linux x64. Standalone reproduction script (`repro-reentrant-guard.mjs`) mirroring the exact guard logic from this PR, run directly with `node` — no monorepo build or test runner required. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node repro-reentrant-guard.mjs ``` **Evidence after fix:** Standalone reproduction script output showing before/after behavior: ```text ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Real Behavior Proof: AsyncLocalStorage Re-entrant Guard Date: 2026-05-18T15:53:46.987Z Node: v24.14.0 | Platform: linux x64 ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Scenario 1: WITHOUT guard (main branch behavior) A handler that re-triggers the same event → infinite loop Triggering command:new:test-session (capped at 10 to avoid hang)... [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#3) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#4) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#5) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#6) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#7) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#8) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#9) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#10) — command:new:test-session Result: handler called 10 times (would be infinite without cap) ⚠️ WITHOUT guard, re-entrant calls amplify without bound Scenario 2: WITH guard (PR branch behavior) Same handler → re-entrant call is correctly blocked Triggering command:new:test-session... [WITH GUARD] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session [WITH GUARD] re-entrant call BLOCKED ✅ (key: command|new|test-session) Result: handler called 1 time(s), re-entrant blocked 1 time(s) ✅ WITH guard, re-entrant calls are prevented Scenario 3: Sequential triggers AFTER first completes → OK Triggering #1... [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session Triggering #2... [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:test-session Triggering #3... [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#3) — command:new:test-session Result: handler called 3 time(s) ✅ Sequential triggers work correctly Scenario 4: Concurrent INDEPENDENT triggers → both delivered (simulates fireAndForgetHook behavior) Firing 2 independent concurrent triggers for message:received:session-a... [CONCURRENT] handler invoked (#2) — message:received:session-a [CONCURRENT] handler invoked (#2) — message:received:session-a Result: handler called 2 time(s) ✅ Independent concurrent triggers are both delivered Scenario 5: Different session keys → not blocked Firing command:new for session-a and session-b concurrently... [CROSS-SESSION] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:session-a [CROSS-SESSION] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:session-b Result: handler called 2 time(s) ✅ Different session keys are independently dispatched ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ SUMMARY ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Without guard (main): Re-entrant loop → 10 calls (capped, would be infinite) With guard (PR branch): Re-entrant blocked → handler called 1x, blocked 1x ✅ Sequential triggers → handler called 3x ✅ Concurrent independent→ handler called 2x ✅ Cross-session → handler called 2x ✅ The AsyncLocalStorage guard prevents re-entrant amplification while preserving all legitimate dispatch patterns. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ``` **Observed result after fix:** Re-entrant calls for the same `type:action:sessionKey` within the same async call chain are correctly blocked (Scenario 2), while independent sequential (Scenario 3), concurrent (Scenario 4), and cross-session (Scenario 5) dispatches all proceed normally. The standalone script confirms the guard behavior matches the production fix. **What was not tested:** - Multi-process concurrent triggers (single-process only) - Performance impact under extreme load (normal usage validated) ## Related - Fixes potential infinite loop when embedded agent turns trigger the same hook type ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-26T14:37:05Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces a re-entrancy guard for `triggerInternalHook` to prevent infinite loops when a hook handler calls `triggerInternalHook` for the same `type:action:sessionKey` combination while the original invocation is still in progress. The guard is implemented as a global `Map` singleton (consistent with the existing `handlers` singleton pattern to survive bundle-splitting) and is always cleaned up in a `finally` block. Key changes: - A `triggerGuard` global singleton is added alongside the existing `handlers` singleton. - `triggerInternalHook` sets a guard key before dispatching handlers and deletes it in `finally`, silently dropping and debug-logging any re-entrant call for the same key. - Four new test cases verify the guard blocks re-entrance while permitting sequential, cross-session, and cross-event-type calls. Minor concerns: - The guard key is formed by joining `type`, `action`, and `sessionKey` with `:`. Because `action` and `sessionKey` are open strings, two distinct triples could theoretically produce the same key if either field contains a colon. Current actions are all colon-free so the practical risk is low. - `clearInternalHooks()` resets the handler registry but not `triggerGuard`. The `finally` block ensures the guard is cleaned up in normal flow, but a fire-and-forget `void triggerInternalHook(...)` call (as seen in existing tests at line 63) could theoretically leave a stale entry that affects subsequent tests.

Confidence Score: 5/5

Safe to merge — the guard is correctly implemented with a finally cleanup, the globalThis singleton pattern is consistent with existing code, and the new tests cover all key behavioral boundaries. The core fix is logically correct: the guard is set synchronously before any await, preventing re-entrant calls from the same session/event combination, and the finally block guarantees cleanup regardless of handler errors. The two flagged items are low-risk P2 suggestions with no realistic failure scenario in current usage. No files require special attention beyond the two P2 style suggestions on src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts.

Important Files Changed

 | Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts | Introduces a global triggerGuard Map singleton and wraps handler dispatch in a try/finally that sets and deletes a type:action:sessionKey guard key. Correctly prevents re-entrant invocations of the same event within a running handler. Two minor concerns: the colon-delimited key could theoretically collide, and clearInternalHooks() does not reset the guard map. | | src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts | Adds four targeted test cases covering the new guard: re-entrant block, sequential allow, different-session allow, and different-event-type allow. Tests are well-structured and cover the key behavioral boundaries of the guard logic. |

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`, line 260-262 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/42ca5913d647f772eeb6d7ee349c12d1596990d0/src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts#L260-L262)) **`clearInternalHooks` does not reset the `triggerGuard`** `clearInternalHooks()` only clears the `handlers` singleton but leaves `triggerGuard` untouched. In normal execution this is fine because the `finally` block always removes the guard key after each call. In tests, however, if a hook is triggered with `void triggerInternalHook(...)` (fire-and-forget — see line 63 of the test file) and the test ends before the handler resolves, the guard entry can outlive the test and cause the next test's first trigger for that key to be silently skipped. Independent concurrent triggers each get their own store (undefined) and proceed normally. Only truly re-entrant calls (handler → `triggerInternalHook` → same key) see the guard in their inherited store and are blocked. This preserves at-least-once delivery semantics while still preventing infinite re-entrant loops. ### P2: Safe key separator Changed from `:` to `\0` (null byte) for the guard key separator. Since `InternalHookEventType` is a closed union (safe), but `action` and `sessionKey` are open strings, null byte eliminates any collision risk. ### Updated tests The existing test suite still covers the re-entrant case. Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a gap where `requesterAgentIdOverride` — already threaded through the `subagent` spawn path — was silently dropped when the ACP (`runtime="acp"`) path was used via `sessions_spawn`. The fix adds the field to `SpawnAcpContext`, propagates it from the tool layer into `spawnAcpDirect`, and makes both `isHeartbeatEnabledForSessionAgent` and `resolveAcpSpawnRequesterState` consult the override before falling back to the parsed session key. - `sessions-spawn-tool.ts`: passes `opts?.requesterAgentIdOverride` into the ACP context (it was already passed into the subagent context on line 205). - `acp-spawn.ts`: adds `requesterAgentIdOverride` to `SpawnAcpContext`; resolves `requesterAgentId` with override priority in both the heartbeat-eligibility helper and the requester-state resolver. - Two regression tests are added: one verifying tool-layer passthrough, one verifying that the override correctly gates implicit parent-stream relay when the overridden agent lacks a heartbeat config.

Confidence Score: 5/5

Safe to merge — minimal, targeted fix that mirrors an already-validated pattern from the subagent path. The change is a pure gap-fill: requesterAgentIdOverride was already correctly handled in subagent-spawn.ts and just wasn't wired into the ACP path. The implementation follows the exact same override-then-fallback pattern used in subagent-spawn.ts:391. Both callsites in acp-spawn.ts are updated consistently, and the two new tests cover the two distinct behaviors being fixed. No logic errors or unintended side-effects were found. No files require special attention.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 56532 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/56532 - Title: memory-lancedb: add configurable timeout/retry for embedding calls - State: OPEN - Author: amittell - Labels: docs, extensions: memory-lancedb, size: M, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🦐 gold shrimp, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: ⏳ waiting on author Body: ```markdown Replaces #56517 (closed, could not reopen). - Add embedding.timeoutMs (default 10s) and embedding.maxRetries (default 1) to memory-lancedb plugin config - Prevents cascading Connection error failures during API rate-limit storms - Also bumps LaunchAgent ThrottleInterval from 1s to 10s ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed**: `memory-lancedb`'s OpenAI-SDK embedding client had no operator-tunable timeout/retry budget. A hung or 5xx-storming embedding backend blocks on the SDK default (600000ms) and stalls every agent turn that triggers auto-recall. This PR lets operators bound it via `embedding.timeoutMs` / `embedding.maxRetries`. Per ClawSweeper review, the bounds now apply ONLY when explicitly configured; unset installs keep the SDK defaults (no upgrade behavior change). - **Real environment tested**: local `openclaw` checkout (upgrade onto current upstream/main) with the bundled `openai` SDK the plugin loads at runtime, exercised against a blackhole TCP endpoint that accepts the connection but never responds (simulating a hung embedding backend). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ```bash # blackhole server + the same OpenAI client the plugin constructs, with the # configured bound applied: node ./embed-timeout-proof.mjs # full script in PR discussion # core: new OpenAI({ baseURL: , timeout: 2000, maxRetries: 1 }) # .embeddings.create({ model, input }) ``` - **Evidence after fix**: captured live from the blackhole run via `node` against the bundled `openai` SDK in `~/.openclaw`/local checkout: ``` blackhole embedding endpoint listening at http://127.0.0.1:60765/v1 (never responds) configured timeoutMs=2000 maxRetries=1: failed after 4520ms — APIConnectionTimeoutError: Request timed out. ``` The configured bound aborts at 2000ms, retries once, and surfaces `APIConnectionTimeoutError` after ~4.5s total — instead of hanging on the SDK default 600000ms. The deployed wiring in `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` now spreads `timeout`/`maxRetries` into the `new OpenAI({...})` constructor only when set (`...(timeoutMs !== undefined ? { timeout: timeoutMs } : {})`), so unconfigured installs retain SDK defaults. - **Observed result after fix**: With `embedding.timeoutMs` set, a hung endpoint fails fast at the configured bound. With it unset, the OpenAI SDK default (600000ms / 2 retries) is preserved unchanged — no regression for installs with slow-but-working endpoints (the upgrade risk ClawSweeper flagged). Config-layer clamping still rejects out-of-range values (`timeoutMs` to [1000, 60000], `maxRetries` to [0, 5]). - **What was not tested**: A real upstream OpenAI 5xx wave (used a local blackhole to deterministically reproduce the hang). Behavior on a partially-responsive endpoint that sends headers then stalls mid-body (the SDK's bodyTimeout governs that path, unchanged by this PR). ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-28T17:18:08Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds configurable `embedding.timeoutMs` (default 10 s) and `embedding.maxRetries` (default 1) to the `memory-lancedb` plugin, propagating both into the OpenAI client constructor and as a per-request override. It also bumps the launchd `ThrottleInterval` from 1 s to 10 s to mitigate rapid crash-loop respawns. **Issues found:** - **Stale test assertion (`index.test.ts` lines 288–292):** The original `toHaveBeenCalledWith(params)` (no second arg) was not removed when the new assertion `toHaveBeenCalledWith(params, { timeout: 10_000 })` was added. Because `embeddingsCreate` is now always called with two arguments, the old assertion will fail — Vitest's `toHaveBeenCalledWith` requires an exact argument match. The old assertion needs to be deleted. **Other notes:** - Passing `timeout` both to the OpenAI client constructor and as a per-request option is harmless (per-request wins); the constructor-level timeout acts as a safety net for any other incidental requests made through the same client instance. - Reducing `maxRetries` from the SDK default (2) to 1 is intentional and well-motivated for rate-limit storm mitigation. - The `ThrottleInterval` increase trades a slightly slower intentional restart for meaningful protection against rapid crash loops — reasonable for a daemon.

- State: OPEN
- Author: kenchen3000
- Labels: P3, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Two related feature requests for the Android (and iOS/macOS) Talk/Voice mode:

## 1. Voice/Talk tab agent switching

Currently the Voice tab is hardcoded to the `main` session (default agent). The Chat tab already has a session picker that lets users switch between agents, but the Voice tab does not.

**Request:** Allow users to select which agent to talk to in the Voice tab, similar to how the Chat tab session picker works.

**Use case:** Users with multiple agents (e.g., IT Director, Data Scientist, Marketing Director) want to have voice conversations with each of them, not just the default agent.

**Current behavior:** Voice tab always sends to `main` session via `chat.send`. Switching sessions in the Chat tab does not affect the Voice tab.

**Expected behavior:** Voice tab respects the selected session/agent, or has its own agent picker.

**Code reference:** `VoiceTabScreen.kt` has no session selection. `TalkModeManager.kt` uses `mainSessionKey` which defaults to `"main"`. The Chat tab's session switcher (`ChatSheetContent.kt`) already demonstrates the pattern.

## 2. Per-agent TTS voice configuration

Currently TTS voice is a global setting (`messages.tts.microsoft.voice` or `talk.voiceId`). All agents share the same voice.

**Request:** Allow per-agent voice configuration so each agent can have a distinct voice identity.

**Use case:** A user running multiple AI agents wants each agent to sound different — e.g., a deep male voice for one, a warm female voice for another. This makes multi-agent setups more intuitive and immersive.

**Proposed config example:**
```json
{
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "talk": { "voiceId": "voice-id-1" }
      },
      {
        "id": "it-director",
        "talk": { "voiceId": "voice-id-2" }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

Or via workspace-level config in each agent's workspace files.

### Impact

These two features together would enable natural voice conversations with multiple AI agents, each with their own voice identity — a significant step toward multi-agent voice interaction.

### Environment

- OpenClaw Android app (built from source, v2026.3.20)
- Gateway on macOS (Mac Mini)
- Multiple agents configured (5+ agents)
```

Yes for the remaining direction, but it needs maintainer UX scoping. Reusing the existing session or agent-selector contract is the maintainable path; adding the requested `talk.voiceId` shape would duplicate the canonical `agents.list[].tts` surface.

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #58765: Feature: Support output dimensionality truncation for local GGUF embedding models
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 58765
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/58765
- Title: Feature: Support output dimensionality truncation for local GGUF embedding models
- State: OPEN
- Author: losz5000
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

The `outputDimensionality` config field currently only works for Gemini embedding-2 models. Local GGUF embedding models (e.g., Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B) always output their full dimension (1024), with no way to truncate.

## Use Case

Many modern embedding models support Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL), where the first N dimensions contain most of the semantic information. For example, Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B outputs 1024 dimensions but the first 256-512 dimensions are sufficient for most retrieval tasks.

Benefits of truncation:
- **50-75% reduction** in vector storage for memory search
- **Faster similarity search** as the index grows
- **Minimal accuracy loss** (<2% for MRL-trained models)

## Proposed Solution

1. Make `outputDimensionality` work for all embedding providers (not just Gemini)
2. After computing the embedding vector, simply `slice(0, N)` before storing
3. When changing the dimension, trigger a full reindex (same as Gemini behavior)

Alternative: Add a separate `truncateDimensions` field under `memorySearch.local` if keeping `outputDimensionality` Gemini-specific is intentional.

## Config Example

```json
{
  "memorySearch": {
    "provider": "local",
    "local": {
      "modelPath": "Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf"
    },
    "outputDimensionality": 512
  }
}
```

## Bonus: Query Instruction Support

Qwen3-Embedding also supports "Instruction Aware" embeddings (prepend a task description to queries for 1-5% improvement). Adding a `queryInstruction` field would be valuable:

```json
{
  "memorySearch": {
    "queryInstruction": "Search for relevant information in intelligence notes and reports"
  }
}
```

## Environment
- OpenClaw v2026.3.24
- macOS ARM64
- Local GGUF embedding: Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-Q8_0
```

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 58775
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/58775
- Title: Bug: google-vertex provider merged into google transport path in 2026.3.28 (regression)
- State: OPEN
- Author: xuanmingguo
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
# Bug: google-vertex provider merged into google transport path in 2026.3.28 (regression)

## Bug Summary
In OpenClaw 2026.3.28, the `google-vertex` provider is incorrectly treated as a Google AI alias by `shouldNormalizeGoogleGenerativeAiProviderConfig`. This causes Vertex AI requests to use the wrong URL format and auth method, breaking the entire `google-vertex` provider.

## Root Cause
The changelog states:
> "Google/models: resolve Gemini 3.1 pro, flash, and flash-lite for all Google provider aliases"

The intent was to unify model ID resolution (e.g., `gemini-3.1-pro` → `gemini-3.1-pro-preview`) across all Google aliases. However, `shouldNormalizeGoogleGenerativeAiProviderConfig` included `google-vertex` in its normalization, which merged not just model ID resolution but also the **transport path and auth method** — this is an over-merge.

## Evidence This Is a Bug (Not Intentional)

1. **`google-vertex` still exists as a separate provider** — if the intent was to merge it, there would be no reason to keep the provider name. Its only purpose is different billing/auth from `google` (AI Studio).

2. **`anthropic-vertex` was NOT merged** — in the same version, `anthropic-vertex` retains independent transport, auth, and stream implementations (`anthropic-vertex-CRO19LKV.js`, `anthropic-vertex-stream.ts`). This confirms Vertex AI paths were not intentionally deprecated.

3. **SDK still supports Vertex AI** — `@google/genai` v1.47.0 fully preserves `vertexai: true`, `project`, `location`, and service account auth. No SDK-side change justifies this.

4. **Environment variables still referenced** — `GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT`, `GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION`, `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` are still read in the code, confirming Vertex AI support was not intentionally removed.

## Impact
- URL format changed from Vertex AI style (`/projects/.../locations/.../publishers/google/models/...`) to Google AI style (`/models/...`)
- Service account auth (`google-vertex` with ADC) replaced by `GEMINI_API_KEY`, making Google Cloud billing credits unusable
- Any `google-vertex/*` model assignment silently routes to the wrong endpoint and fails

## Expected Behavior
`google-vertex` should use Vertex AI transport/auth (service account ADC, Vertex AI URL format) while still benefiting from unified model ID resolution.

## Workaround
Currently setting `models.providers.google-vertex.api = "google-generative-ai"` in config forces the correct adapter, but this loses the Vertex AI-specific URL/auth benefits and is a hack rather than a fix.

## Environment
- OpenClaw: 2026.3.28 (f9b1079)
- Provider config: `google-vertex` with service account ADC
- Models affected: `google-vertex/gemini-3.1-pro-preview` and all `google-vertex/*` models

Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster
```
title: Bug: google-vertex provider merged into google transport path in 2026.3.28 (regression)

Body:
```markdown
# [Bug]: microsoft-foundry provider selects Claude deployments but routes them through OpenAI Foundry endpoints

## Summary

The built-in `microsoft-foundry` provider can discover/select Anthropic Claude deployments from Azure AI Foundry, but it still normalizes them onto the OpenAI-compatible runtime path (`/openai/v1`, `chat/completions` or `responses`) instead of the Anthropic Foundry path (`/anthropic/v1/messages`).

This makes Claude deployments appear selectable in onboarding/config, but real requests fail at runtime (commonly 404).

## Why this looks like a bug in the built-in provider

Microsoft's Claude-on-Foundry docs expect:

- base URL: `https://<resource>.services.ai.azure.com/anthropic`
- request path: `/anthropic/v1/messages`
- API shape: Anthropic Messages API
- auth:
  - API key: `x-api-key`
  - Entra ID: `Authorization: Bearer <token>`
- Anthropic header: `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01`

But the built-in `microsoft-foundry` provider currently behaves like an OpenAI-compatible Foundry adapter:

- `dist/provider-CbANfJPO.js`
  - `capabilities: { providerFamily: "openai" }`
- `dist/shared-Jirm7-bE.js`
  - Foundry API resolution only supports `openai-completions` / `openai-responses`
  - endpoint normalization/building rewrites to `.../openai/v1`
- `dist/onboard-BZJYM71H.js`
  - connection test only calls `/chat/completions` or `/responses`
- `dist/runtime-BEDfp2bA.js`
  - Entra token refresh is wired, but the rebuilt base URL still comes from the OpenAI-style Foundry base URL builder

So the provider can successfully:

- authenticate with Azure CLI / Entra ID
- list Foundry resources
- list deployments
- let the user select Claude deployments

but then still send the wrong protocol/route for Anthropic deployments.

## Repro

1. Run OpenClaw onboarding/configure.
2. Select **Microsoft Foundry**.
3. Choose **Azure CLI login** (`az login` already completed).
4. Select a Claude deployment from Azure AI Foundry, e.g. `claude-opus-4-6`.
5. Use that model in a real run.

## Observed behavior

The configured model is routed through OpenAI-compatible Foundry endpoints instead of Anthropic Foundry endpoints, and requests fail (for example with 404).

## Expected behavior

One of these should happen:

### Option A: proper Anthropic support in `microsoft-foundry`

If the selected deployment is Anthropic/Claude, the provider should:

- support `api: "anthropic-messages"`
- build base URL as `https://<resource>.services.ai.azure.com/anthropic`
- call `/v1/messages`
- send `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01`
- use:
  - `x-api-key` for API key auth
  - `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for Entra ID auth

### Option B: guardrail until support exists

If Anthropic deployments are not yet supported by the built-in provider, onboarding should **not** offer them as selectable deployments, or should clearly warn that only OpenAI-compatible Foundry APIs are currently supported.

## Actual user impact

This is especially confusing because:

- the Azure CLI login flow works
- resource + deployment discovery works
- Claude deployments are shown as selectable
- the runtime failure only appears later when making actual requests

So it looks like configuration succeeded even though the provider/runtime protocol is wrong for that deployment family.

## Related but distinct issue

- #32179 is about **custom** Azure Foundry Anthropic provider streaming/SSE parsing.
- This issue is about the built-in `microsoft-foundry` provider selecting Anthropic deployments but routing them via the wrong API family.

## Workaround

Current workaround is to use a **custom provider** pointed at the Anthropic Foundry endpoint instead of the built-in `microsoft-foundry` provider, e.g.:

- base URL: `https://<resource>.services.ai.azure.com/anthropic`
- API: `anthropic-messages`

API-key auth works most cleanly there today.

Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster
```
title: [Bug]: microsoft-foundry provider selects Claude deployments but routes them through OpenAI Foundry endpoints

Body:
```markdown
[Feature]: Per-DM/per-chat default ACP binding for auto-spawning ACP sessions on new topics

## Problem

When using Telegram DM forum topics with ACP agents (Claude Code, Codex, etc.), every new topic requires manually running `/acp spawn claude` or asking the main agent to spawn an ACP session. There is no way to configure a DM chat so that new topics automatically bind to an ACP agent.

## Proposed solution

Add a `defaultAcp` config option to `TelegramDirectConfig` (and potentially `TelegramGroupConfig`) that auto-spawns an ACP session when a new topic/thread is created.

### Config example

```json5
{
  "channels": {
    "telegram": {
      "direct": {
        "63448508": {
          "defaultAcp": {
            "agent": "claude",
            "mode": "persistent",
            "cwd": "/workspace/default"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Behavior

1. User creates a new topic in Telegram DM with the bot
2. On first message in the new topic, OpenClaw detects no existing ACP binding
3. OpenClaw auto-spawns an ACP session using `defaultAcp` config
4. The topic is bound to that ACP session
5. All subsequent messages in the topic route directly to the ACP agent
6. Topics with explicit per-topic config (`topics.<id>.agentId`) should override `defaultAcp`

### Scope

- `TelegramDirectConfig.defaultAcp` - for DM topics
- `TelegramGroupConfig.defaultAcp` - for group forum topics
- Could also be a top-level `TelegramAccountConfig.defaultAcp` for account-wide default

### Alternatives considered

- **Static bindings per topic:** Works but requires manual config for each topic ID upfront
- **Main agent with auto-spawn system prompt:** Wastes a main agent turn just to spawn ACP
- **`/acp spawn` in every new topic:** Manual friction, defeats the purpose of topics as lightweight sessions

### Related

- Thread bindings: `threadBindings.spawnAcpSessions`
- Existing ACP binding model: `bindings[].type="acp"`
- Per-topic agentId routing: `topics.<id>.agentId`

```

github_context:
GitHub item:

- Type: github_issue
- Number: 62109
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/62109
- Title: Interactive runs fail with auth-style 403 when custom OpenAI-compatible provider baseUrl uses Unicode/IDN or punycode hostname, but ASCII hostname/IP works
- State: OPEN
- Author: nboody
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-info, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦐 gold shrimp

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

OpenClaw interactive runs (Web UI and local TUI / embedded path) fail with:

- `403 Your request was blocked.`
- `failoverReason: "auth"`
- `profileFailureReason: "auth"`

when a custom OpenAI-compatible provider uses either:

- a Unicode/IDN hostname in `baseUrl`
- or the punycode form of that same hostname

The same backend works when accessed via:

- ASCII hostname
- public IP + dedicated `/v1`-only proxy port

This suggests an issue in OpenClaw's handling of non-ASCII-origin hostnames (including punycode forms) in the interactive/embedded run path.

---

### Environment

- OpenClaw version: `2026.4.5`
- Host: Linux
- Surface:
  - Web UI
  - local TUI
- Provider type:
  - custom OpenAI-compatible provider
  - `api: openai-completions`

---

### Affected behavior

With a provider config like this:

```json
{
  "models": {
    "providers": {
      "custom_provider": {
        "baseUrl": "https://<unicode-idn-domain>/v1",
        "api": "openai-completions",
        "apiKey": "<redacted>",
        "models": [
          {
            "id": "gpt-5.4",
            "name": "gpt-5.4",
            "contextWindow": 200000,
            "maxTokens": 8192
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": {
        "primary": "custom_provider/gpt-5.4"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

interactive runs fail before a usable assistant reply is produced.

Relevant logs:

```text
error: 403 Your request was blocked.
failoverReason: auth
profileFailureReason: auth
provider: custom_provider
model: gpt-5.4
```

---

### Key observation

The backend itself is healthy.

Direct manual requests to the same backend succeed:

```bash
curl https://<unicode-idn-domain>/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <redacted>' \
  --data '{"model":"gpt-5.4","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"reply with exactly: ok"}],"max_tokens":8}'
```

This returns HTTP 200 and `ok`.

The same is true for the punycode hostname and for an ASCII hostname.

So the failure is not simply "backend unavailable".

---

### What was tested

#### 1. Different backends
Two different OpenAI-compatible backends were tested:

- a LiteLLM-based backend
- a different OAuth-backed OpenAI-compatible backend

Manual requests succeeded against both backends.

#### 2. Unicode hostname
Using a Unicode/IDN hostname in `baseUrl` caused interactive runs to fail with auth-style 403.

Example shape:

- `https://<unicode-idn-domain>/v1`

#### 3. Punycode hostname
Using the punycode form of the same hostname also failed in interactive runs.

Example shape:

- `https://<punycode-domain>/v1`

Manual `chat/completions` requests still succeeded through this hostname.

#### 4. Public IP + `/v1`-only gateway
A dedicated public proxy port exposing only `/v1/*` was created:

- `http://<public-ip>:<port>/v1`

Using that IP-based URL in the provider made Web UI / TUI interactive runs succeed.

#### 5. ASCII hostname
Using an unrelated ASCII-only hostname also worked:

- `https://<ascii-domain>/v1`

---

### Minimal conclusion

The failure correlates with using a hostname derived from an IDN domain in provider `baseUrl`.

- Unicode hostname: fails in interactive run path
- punycode hostname: also fails
- unrelated ASCII hostname: works
- public IP + port: works

---

### Expected behavior

OpenClaw should handle provider `baseUrl` hostnames consistently regardless of whether the configured hostname is:

- Unicode/IDN form
- punycode form
- plain ASCII hostname
- public IP

If manual requests to the backend succeed, the interactive run path should not fail early with a local auth-style 403 purely because the configured hostname is Unicode or punycode.

---

### Actual behavior

When `baseUrl` contains either a Unicode/IDN hostname or the punycode form of that hostname, interactive runs fail locally with auth-style 403:

```text
403 Your request was blocked.
failoverReason: auth
profileFailureReason: auth
```

even though the same backend is otherwise reachable and returns valid chat completions.

---

### Workaround

Avoid Unicode/IDN-derived hostname forms in provider `baseUrl`.

Use either:

- an unrelated ASCII hostname
- or a public IP + dedicated `/v1`-only proxy

Example working shape:

```json
"baseUrl": "https://<ascii-domain>/v1"
```

or:

```json
"baseUrl": "http://<public-ip>:<port>/v1"
```

instead of:

```json
"baseUrl": "https://<unicode-idn-domain>/v1"
```

or:

```json
"baseUrl": "https://<punycode-domain>/v1"
```

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #62769: [Feature]: Support ACP configured bindings for Telegram DMs (not just groups/topics)
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 62769
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/62769
- Title: [Feature]: Support ACP configured bindings for Telegram DMs (not just groups/topics)
- State: OPEN
- Author: richardmqq
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Telegram ACP configured bindings (`bindings[]` with `type: "acp"`) only work for group chats and forum topics, not for direct messages (DMs). This prevents using persistent ACP sessions in Telegram DM conversations with dedicated agent bots.

## Root Cause

In `normalizeTelegramAcpConversationId()`, the function explicitly rejects any `chatId` that does not start with `"-"` (i.e., group chat IDs):

```javascript
function normalizeTelegramAcpConversationId(conversationId) {
    const parsed = parseTelegramTopicConversation({ conversationId });
    if (!parsed || !parsed.chatId.startsWith("-")) return null;
    // ...
}
```

This means DM peer IDs (positive integers like `6689123501`) are filtered out, and the binding is silently ignored.

Similarly, `matchTelegramAcpConversation()` checks `incoming.chatId.startsWith("-")`, which also rejects DM conversations.

## Expected Behavior

A configured binding like this should work for Telegram DMs:

```json
{
  "type": "acp",
  "agentId": "claude",
  "match": {
    "channel": "telegram",
    "accountId": "claude",
    "peer": { "kind": "direct", "id": "6689123501" }
  },
  "acp": { "mode": "persistent" }
}
```

When a user sends a message to the dedicated agent bot in DM, it should route through the ACP binding and create/resume an ACP harness session, just like it does for group topics.

## Actual Behavior

- The binding passes `openclaw config validate` ✅
- But at runtime, `normalizeTelegramAcpConversationId()` returns `null` for DM peer IDs
- The binding is silently ignored
- Messages fall through to native embedded runtime instead of ACP harness

## Use Case

We run dedicated Telegram bots for different ACP agents (Claude Code, Codex, etc.). Each bot has its own `accountId`. Users DM these bots expecting to interact with the actual ACP harness (e.g., Claude Code CLI). Without DM binding support, there is no way to persistently bind a Telegram DM conversation to an ACP session through config.

Currently, the only workaround is `/acp spawn` in a group topic, which defeats the purpose of having dedicated DM bots.

## Additional Context

- Gateway restart can cause existing ACP session metadata to be lost from `sessions.json`, making the DM → ACP routing even less reliable
- `agents.list[].runtime.type: "acp"` does NOT automatically route DM messages through ACP — it only provides defaults for `/acp spawn`
- The `acpManager.resolveSession()` check in `agentCommandInternal()` returns `kind: "none"` for DM sessions without `.acp` metadata, falling through to native embedded runtime

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: latest (2026.4.5+)
- Channel: Telegram
- ACP backend: acpx (embedded)
```

**Summary**
Keep open: current main and the latest release still only compile Telegram ACP configured bindings from topic-shaped group IDs, while the related direct-chat fix remains an open draft PR with prior review blockers.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection. Configure a top-level Telegram ACP binding with `match.peer.kind: "direct"` and a positive `match.peer.id`; current main passes that ID into Telegram configured-binding compilation, where the adapter returns null before registration.

**Next step**
No separate repair job: the open draft PR already targets this behavior, so a parallel issue repair would duplicate that fix path.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Land or replace https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68537 with a narrow Telegram bindings-adapter change that accepts plain positive DM IDs, preserves topic/group rejection rules, and updates focused docs/tests.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, by source inspection. Configure a top-level Telegram ACP binding with `match.peer.kind: "direct"` and a positive `match.peer.id`; current main passes that ID into Telegram configured-binding compilation, where the adapter returns null before registration.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes for the requested direction, but it is not implemented on main. The maintainable fix belongs in Telegram configured-binding normalization and inbound matching, with coverage for plain positive DM IDs and rejection of invalid topic-shaped IDs.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: This is a valid Telegram ACP routing improvement with a clear source path and limited channel-specific blast radius.
- `impact:message-loss`: The reported messages are routed to the wrong runtime path instead of the configured ACP binding.
- `impact:session-state`: The requested behavior is about creating or resuming the intended persistent ACP session for Telegram DM conversations.

What I checked:

- **current_adapter_rejects_direct_ids:** `normalizeTelegramAcpConversationId()` parses only Telegram topic-shaped conversation IDs and returns null unless the parsed chat ID starts with `-`, so a plain positive DM ID cannot compile as a Telegram ACP configured binding target. ([`extensions/telegram/src/channel.ts:296`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/f07c87405c30/extensions/telegram/src/channel.ts#L296), [f07c87405c30](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f07c87405c30))
- **inbound_match_repeats_topic_gate:** `matchTelegramAcpConversation()` uses the same normalizer and also rejects incoming conversations unless topic parsing succeeds with a negative chat ID. ([`extensions/telegram/src/channel.ts:307`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/f07c87405c30/extensions/telegram/src/channel.ts#L307), [f07c87405c30](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f07c87405c30))
- **direct_messages_reach_configured_binding_resolution:** Telegram DMs resolve `peerId` via `resolveTelegramDirectPeerId()` and pass that value as `conversationId` into `resolveConfiguredBindingRoute()`, putting the adapter rejection on the reported runtime path. ([`extensions/telegram/src/conversation-route.ts:37`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/f07c87405c30/extensions/telegram/src/conversation-route.ts#L37), [f07c87405c30](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f07c87405c30))
- **null_compiled_targets_are_skipped:** The configured-binding compiler skips a binding when the channel provider returns a null target, which is what the Telegram adapter returns for plain positive DM IDs today. ([`src/channels/plugins/configured-binding-compiler.ts:137`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/f07c87405c30/src/channels/plugins/configured-binding-compiler.ts#L137), [f07c87405c30](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f07c87405c30))
- **schema_accepts_direct_peer_bindings:** The shared binding schema accepts `peer.kind: "direct"` and ACP bindings only require a concrete `match.peer.id`, so config validation can pass before Telegram runtime matching drops the binding. ([`src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts:21`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/f07c87405c30/src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts#L21), [f07c87405c30](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f07c87405c30))
- **current_docs_remain_topic_scoped:** Current Telegram and ACP docs still describe persistent Telegram ACP bindings as forum-topic/topic-qualified IDs, not plain DM IDs. Public docs: [`docs/channels/telegram.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/telegram). ([`docs/channels/telegram.md:661`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/telegram), [f07c87405c30](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f07c87405c30))

Likely related people:

- **dutifulbob:** Opened the merged persistent ACP binding PR that introduced Telegram topic-scoped configured ACP bindings and touched the affected Telegram, ACP docs, and config surfaces. (role: introduced behavior and adjacent feature owner; confidence: high; commits: [6a705a37f2cf](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/6a705a37f2cf856a6237eac430210859914f67d7); files: `docs/channels/telegram.md`, `docs/tools/acp-agents.md`, `src/config/config.acp-binding-cutover.test.ts`)
- **osolmaz:** Authored many commits inside the persistent binding and plugin-seam PRs, including shared binding semantics, config validation, and configured-binding routing cleanup now being extended. (role: adjacent feature contributor; confidence: high; commits: [d06a0157cad9](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/d06a0157cad94086c2fa4d5e29e9ccd92d40982d), [224ba7e8fee0](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/224ba7e8fee0e300e3f1d7835ff2608b130130ff), [e0df5fc1b8bb](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/e0df5fc1b8bbd27791a923f241ffd069b7a9cb08); files: `src/channels/plugins/configured-binding-compiler.ts`, `src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts`, `docs/tools/acp-agents.md`)
- **TSHOGX:** Authored the recent Telegram topic parser guard directly adjacent to the DM-vs-topic parsing boundary involved in this fix path. (role: recent adjacent contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [1d1f3e14b9c4](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1d1f3e14b9c45183dc02f5e3384688b7f0f376f9); files: `extensions/telegram/src/topic-conversation.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/topic-conversation.test.ts`)

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 62966 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/62966 - Title: feat: expose replyToId in inbound_claim hook metadata - State: OPEN - Author: david-lihangyuan - Labels: size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Expose `replyToId` and `replyToBody` in the `inbound_claim` plugin hook metadata, enabling plugins to implement reply-aware message routing. ## Motivation We're building a plugin that routes user replies to heartbeat messages back to the heartbeat session (instead of the main conversation session). This enables natural async conversations with heartbeat — user sees a heartbeat message, replies to it, and the reply goes to the right context. Currently, `ReplyToId` and `ReplyToBody` exist in the Telegram bot context (`FinalizedMsgContext`) and are used to build the agent's inbound metadata (`inbound-meta.ts`), but they are not propagated to the plugin hook layer. Greptile Summary

This PR adds `agents.defaults.memoryInjection` (`"full"` | `"core-only"` | `"recall-only"`) to control whether `MEMORY.md` is injected into the bootstrap context, and `agents.defaults.memorySearch.autoRecall` to automatically prepend semantically-relevant memory hits before each prompt build. Schema, types, Zod validation, labels, and help text are all updated consistently, and the `memory-core` extension registers the new `before_prompt_build` hook with safe HTML-escaping for injected memory snippets. Two P2 concerns worth revisiting before wider adoption: (1) `\"core-only\"` and `\"full\"` are currently identical in the implementation — `applyMemoryInjectionFilter` branches only on `\"recall-only\"`, contrary to the docs that imply `\"core-only\"` enforces truncation limits while `\"full\"` doesn't; (2) the startup warning for `recall-only`-without-`autoRecall` fires only when *no* agent has `autoRecall` enabled, which could silently leave per-agent gaps uncovered.

Confidence Score: 5/5

Safe to merge; all remaining findings are P2 style/clarity issues. The core logic is sound: `recall-only` filtering, autoRecall resolution with clamping, safe HTML escaping of injected memory, and graceful error degradation all work correctly. Both noted issues are semantic (mode equivalence) and warning-scope (partial coverage), neither of which causes wrong data, data loss, or runtime errors. src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts — `core-only` vs `full` equivalence; extensions/memory-core/src/auto-recall.ts — warning scope for mixed autoRecall configurations.

Vulnerabilities

 - Memory snippet injection in `auto-recall.ts` HTML-escapes `&`, `<`, `>`, `"`, and `'` before inserting into the `` XML context block, preventing untrusted memory content from escaping the boundary or injecting arbitrary prompt instructions. - No secrets, credentials, or sensitive paths are exposed in the new code paths. - The `before_prompt_build` hook reads config fresh per-turn via `api.runtime.config.loadConfig()`, which is consistent with existing patterns and does not introduce new attack surfaces.

Path: src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts Line: 43-53 Comment: **`core-only` and `full` modes are currently identical** `applyMemoryInjectionFilter` only gates on `"recall-only"`, so `"core-only"` passes through unchanged — the same as `"full"`. The docs describe them differently ("core-only keeps it inside the normal bootstrap size limits"), but the code applies identical truncation in both cases. Users who set `"core-only"` get no behavioural difference from `"full"`, which could cause confusion.
